kpis.name: Absenteeism Rate
kpis.description: The frequency and extent to which employees are absent from work, excluding pre-approved leave like vacations. High absenteeism can indicate employee dissatisfaction, burnout, health issues, or a problematic work environment. Consistently monitoring this metric ensures that employee well-being is prioritized, and productivity is maintained.
kpis.formula: (Number of Unplanned Absent Days / Total Number of Working Days) x 100
kpis.example: If employees took a total of 50 unplanned absent days over 1000 working days, the absenteeism rate would be 5%.

kpis.name: Accepted Pull Requests
kpis.description: Measures the number of pull requests that have been merged or accepted into the project. It indicates the project’s activity level and the community’s contribution to project development.
kpis.formula: Number of Pull Requests Accepted
kpis.example: If 400 out of 500 submitted pull requests are accepted, the total is 400.

kpis.name: Account Churn Rate
kpis.description: Indicates the rate at which accounts are lost over a certain period, signaling the need for improved retention strategies.
kpis.formula: (Number of Accounts Lost / Total Accounts at Start of Period) * 100
kpis.example: With 5 lost accounts from 100, churn rate is (5 / 100) * 100 = 5%.

kpis.name: Account Conversion Rate
kpis.description: The rate at which prospects or leads are converted into active accounts, assessing the effectiveness of the sales process.
kpis.formula: (Number of New Accounts / Number of Prospects) * 100
kpis.example: For 50 new accounts from 200 prospects, the conversion rate is (50 / 200) * 100 = 25%.

kpis.name: Account Coverage Ratio
kpis.description: Measures the ratio of accounts to account managers, impacting the level of personalized service provided.
kpis.formula: Number of Accounts / Number of Account Managers
kpis.example: If there are 200 accounts for 10 managers, the ratio is 200 / 10 = 20.

kpis.name: Account Engagement Level
kpis.description: Assesses the depth of an account's interaction with the company's services or products, which can be an indicator of account health. A higher score denotes better engagement, essential for customer satisfaction and retention.
kpis.formula: (Number of Interactions + Number of Meetings + Value of Interactions) / Number of Accounts
kpis.example: If there are 50 accounts with 200 interactions, 100 meetings, and a value rating of 400, the Account Engagement Level would be (200+100+400)/50, resulting in a level of 14.

kpis.name: Account Profitability Index
kpis.description: Evaluates the profitability of individual accounts, considering revenues and associated costs to serve the account.
kpis.formula: (Revenue from Account - Cost to Serve Account) / Revenue from Account
kpis.example: If an account generates $10,000 and costs $6,000 to serve, the index is ($10,000 - $6,000) / $10,000 = 0.4.

kpis.name: Account Satisfaction Index
kpis.description: Derived from customer feedback, measures satisfaction levels of clients with the account management services.
kpis.formula: Average of Account Satisfaction Survey Scores
kpis.example: If survey scores are 8, 9, and 8 out of 10, satisfaction index is (8 + 9 + 8) / 3 = 8.3.

kpis.name: Accounts Payable Turnover
kpis.description: How often a company pays off its suppliers relative to the amount of credit extended, often annually. Indicates how efficiently a company is paying its suppliers.
kpis.formula: Cost of Goods Sold (COGS) / Average Accounts Payable
kpis.example: If annual COGS is $500,000 and average accounts payable is $50,000, the turnover rate is 10 times/year.

kpis.name: Acid Test (Quick Ratio)
kpis.description: Evaluates a company's short-term liquidity, discounting inventory. Similar to the current ratio but excludes inventory from current assets. It offers a more stringent measure of short-term liquidity, ensuring that a company can meet its immediate obligations even without selling its inventory.
kpis.formula: (Current Assets - Inventory) / Current Liabilities
kpis.example: If a company has current assets of $1,000,000 (of which $200,000 is inventory) and current liabilities of $500,000, its quick ratio is 1.6:1.

kpis.name: Active Remote Working Agreements
kpis.description: Represents the total number of employees who have agreements in place that allow them to work remotely, either part-time or full-time. This metric reflects a company's adaptation to modern work trends. It can be used to measure the organization's capacity for remote work and its willingness to support diverse working styles.
kpis.formula: Total Number of Active Remote Working Agreements in Place
kpis.example: If 120 employees in a company have active remote working agreements, the value for this KPI would be 120.

kpis.name: Actual vs. Budgeted Cost of Hire
kpis.description: Compares the actual cost of hiring new employees to the budgeted or projected cost. It allows HR departments to assess their efficiency in the hiring process and adjust future budgets based on past spending. Discrepancies can lead to refining recruitment strategies or reallocating budget.
kpis.formula: Actual Cost of Hire - Budgeted Cost of Hire
kpis.example: If the actual cost of hire is $10,000 and the budgeted amount was $8,000, the discrepancy is $2,000.

kpis.name: Ad Conversion Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of users who take a desired action after clicking on an ad. It indicates the effectiveness of an ad or landing page in achieving its goal.
kpis.formula: (Number of conversions / Total clicks) x 100
kpis.example: If out of 100 ad clicks, 20 users sign up for a newsletter, the Conversion Rate is 20%.

kpis.name: Ad Engagement Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of users who engage with an ad, beyond just viewing it. Provides insights into how interactive and appealing the ad is to the audience.
kpis.formula: (Number of engagements / Total impressions) x 100
kpis.example: If an ad receives 200 engagements (e.g., likes, shares, comments) from 1,000 impressions, the Engagement Rate is 20%.

kpis.name: Ad Frequency
kpis.description: The average number of times a person is exposed to an advertisement. Provides an understanding of how often the audience sees or hears the ad, which can influence recall and recognition.
kpis.formula: Total Impressions / Reach
kpis.example: If an ad has 1 million impressions and a reach of 500,000, the frequency is 2.

kpis.name: Ad Position or Average Ad Rank
kpis.description: The average position of an ad in search engine results or on a webpage. Higher positions generally lead to more visibility, clicks, and conversions.
kpis.formula: Average rank based on auction metrics in platforms like Google Ads.
kpis.example: An ad appearing third on a Google search result page has an ad position of 3.

kpis.name: Ad Quality Score
kpis.description: A score provided by platforms like Google Ads, considering factors like CTR, relevance, and landing page quality. Affects ad rank and cost. Higher quality scores can result in lower costs and better ad positions.
kpis.formula: Provided by the advertising platform (e.g., Google Ads).
kpis.example: An ad with a high CTR, relevant keywords, and a high-quality landing page may receive a Quality Score of 9/10.

kpis.name: Ad Reach
kpis.description: The total number of unique individuals exposed to an advertisement.
kpis.formula: Total number of unique viewers/readers/listeners exposed at least once to the advertisement.
kpis.example: If 500,000 people viewed a TV commercial, the reach is 500,000.

kpis.name: Ad Recall
kpis.description: The percentage of audience members who can recall an advertisement after a certain period. Tests the memorability and effectiveness of the ad.
kpis.formula: (Number of people who recall the ad / Total number of people surveyed) x 100
kpis.example: If 250 out of 1,000 surveyed people remember an ad, the Ad Recall is 25%.

kpis.name: Ad Share of Voice
kpis.description: The ad's share of advertising exposure in its category. Provides a measure of the brand's presence and visibility in comparison to competitors.
kpis.formula: (Brand's Ad Expenditure / Total Market Ad Expenditure in the same media) x 100
kpis.example: If a brand spends $500,000 on TV ads in a market where competitors collectively spend $5 million, the brand's SOV is 10%.

kpis.name: Ad Spend Return on Investment
kpis.description: The return gained from the advertising spend. Determines the profitability and effectiveness of advertising campaigns.
kpis.formula: [(Net Profit from Ad Campaign - Cost of Ad Campaign) / Cost of Ad Campaign] x 100
kpis.example: If an ad campaign costs $1,000 and generates $5,000 in sales, the net profit is $4,000. The ROI is 400%.

kpis.name: Administration Cost per Employee
kpis.description: The average cost incurred by the HR department in managing administrative tasks for each employee. Understanding this value helps companies optimize HR-related expenses and determine if there is room for efficiency improvements in HR processes or if additional resources are needed.
kpis.formula: Total HR Administrative Costs / Total Number of Employees
kpis.example: If HR administrative costs for a year are $100,000 and there are 200 employees, the Administration Cost per Employee would be $500.

kpis.name: Affiliate Conversion Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of leads from affiliates that convert into sales or a desired action. Measures the effectiveness of your affiliate partnerships.
kpis.formula: (Number of sales via affiliates / Total affiliate-generated leads) x 100
kpis.example: If 50 sales were made from 500 leads generated by affiliates, the conversion rate is 10%.

kpis.name: Affiliate Program Cost
kpis.description: The total cost of running the affiliate program, including commissions, platform fees, and other related expenses. Helps in assessing the profitability and sustainability of the affiliate program.
kpis.formula: Sum of all expenses related to the affiliate program
kpis.example: If you paid $3,000 in commissions, $500 in platform fees, and $500 in other expenses, the total affiliate program cost is $4,000.

kpis.name: Affiliate Revenue Share
kpis.description: The percentage of total revenue that is attributed to affiliate sales. Indicates the significance of the affiliate channel to overall revenue.
kpis.formula: (Revenue from affiliate sales / Total revenue) x 100
kpis.example: If $10,000 of a $100,000 monthly revenue came from affiliates, the affiliate revenue share is 10%.

kpis.name: Agent Adherence To Schedule
kpis.description: The percentage of time agents adhere to their planned work schedules. Adherence to schedules ensures that there are enough agents available to handle incoming calls, especially during peak times. Poor adherence can lead to longer wait times for customers and inefficiencies in staffing.
kpis.formula: (Total scheduled working time adhered to by agents / Total scheduled working time) x 100
kpis.example: If agents were scheduled to work for a total of 500 hours and they adhered to 480 hours, the adherence rate would be 96%.

kpis.name: Agent Email Handling Time
kpis.description: The average time an agent spends on an email, from reading it to sending a response. Understanding the time agents spend per email can offer insights into their efficiency, the complexity of issues, or the effectiveness of their tools and resources.
kpis.formula: Total time spent handling emails / Number of emails handled
kpis.example: If agents spent a total of 300 minutes handling 60 emails, the Agent Email Handling Time is 5 minutes.

kpis.name: Agent Satisfaction Rate
kpis.description: The level of satisfaction agents express about their roles, usually gathered through regular surveys. Satisfied agents are more likely to provide better customer service, stay in their roles longer, and promote a positive workplace culture.
kpis.formula: Average score given by agents in satisfaction surveys
kpis.example: If the combined scores from 10 agents amount to 42 on a scale of 1 to 5, the average agent satisfaction rate is 4.2.

kpis.name: Agent Utilization Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of an agent's working time that is spent handling customer interactions. High utilization can indicate efficiency but may also point to potential agent burnout if sustained.
kpis.formula: (Time spent handling interactions / Total working time) x 100
kpis.example: If an agent spends 6 hours of an 8-hour shift handling interactions, the utilization rate is 75%.

kpis.name: Aging Accounts Receivable
kpis.description: A report or schedule detailing the lengths of time that invoices have been outstanding, in days. Aging helps companies identify which invoices are overdue for payment. This can help with collection efforts and assess the effectiveness of credit policies.
kpis.formula: Typically presented as a table breaking down unpaid customer invoices by date ranges (e.g., 0-30 days, 31-60 days, etc.).
kpis.example: An aging report might show $10,000 in invoices are 0-30 days old, $5,000 are 31-60 days old, and so on.

kpis.name: AI Compliance Score
kpis.description: Assesses the AI model's adherence to ethical guidelines, regulatory standards, and best practices in AI development. This is increasingly important for ethical and responsible AI use.
kpis.formula: Compliance Assessment Score
kpis.example: A model that meets all ethical guidelines and regulatory requirements might score 95/100 in AI compliance.

kpis.name: API Usage Growth
kpis.description: API Usage Growth tracks the increase in API usage over time, highlighting trends in adoption and reliance on the API. It’s crucial for understanding the API’s market penetration and long-term viability.
kpis.formula: ((API Requests in Current Period - API Requests in Previous Period) / API Requests in Previous Period) * 100
kpis.example: If API requests grow from 10,000 to 15,000 in a month, the growth is ((15,000 - 10,000) / 10,000) * 100 = 50%.

kpis.name: App Install Rate
kpis.description: App Install Rate measures the percentage of users who install the app after visiting the app's landing page or viewing an ad. It's a crucial metric indicating the effectiveness of the app's marketing and its initial appeal. A high install rate suggests successful marketing and compelling app presentation, while a low rate might indicate the need for improved marketing strategies or app store optimization.
kpis.formula: (Number of Installs / Number of Visitors or Ad Views) * 100
kpis.example: If 1,000 people view an ad for the app, and 100 install it, the App Install Rate is (100/1000)*100 = 10%.

kpis.name: App Load Speed
kpis.description: App Load Speed measures the time taken for the app to start up and become usable. Faster load times contribute to a positive user experience, while slower load times can lead to frustration and increased bounce rates.
kpis.formula: Time from App Launch to Usability
kpis.example: If an app takes 3 seconds to load to a usable state, the App Load Speed is 3 seconds.

kpis.name: App Store Ratings and Reviews
kpis.description: App Store Ratings and Reviews aggregate the overall user ratings and feedback on the app store. High ratings and positive reviews can enhance app visibility and credibility, influencing new user downloads. This metric reflects user satisfaction and perceived app quality.
kpis.formula: Average Rating Score and Qualitative Review Analysis
kpis.example: An app with an average rating of 4.5 out of 5 stars, coupled with positive user reviews, indicates high user satisfaction.

kpis.name: App Uninstall Rate
kpis.description: App Uninstall Rate tracks the percentage of users who uninstall the app after downloading it. This metric helps in understanding user satisfaction and app performance issues. A high uninstall rate may indicate problems with app functionality, user experience, or unmet user expectations.
kpis.formula: (Number of Uninstalls / Number of Installs) * 100
kpis.example: If out of 1,000 installs, 200 users uninstall the app, the Uninstall Rate is (200/1000)*100 = 20%.

kpis.name: Applications Per API
kpis.description: Tracks the number of distinct applications or systems that utilize the API. This metric reflects the API’s versatility and its integration capabilities across different platforms and use cases.
kpis.formula: Count of Applications Using the API
kpis.example: If 100 different applications use the API, the count is 100.

kpis.name: Appointment Setting Rate
kpis.description: The success rate at which sales calls or contacts result in setting an appointment, indicating effectiveness of initial engagement.
kpis.formula: (Number of Appointments Set / Number of Calls or Contacts Made) * 100
kpis.example: If 300 calls lead to 30 appointments, the setting rate is (30/300)*100 = 10%.

kpis.name: Audience Engagement
kpis.description: The degree to which viewers/readers/listeners engage with the advertisement, often measured by direct responses, calls, or inquiries. Shows the level of interest and action taken by the audience after exposure to the advertisement.
kpis.formula: Total number of engagements (calls, inquiries, coupon redemptions, etc.)
kpis.example: If 1,000 people responded to a direct mail campaign out of 100,000 recipients, there were 1,000 engagements.

kpis.name: Audit Completion Time
kpis.description: The time taken to complete a financial audit, in days. Efficiency in audit processes can reduce business interruptions and expedite any necessary corrective actions.
kpis.formula: End date of the audit - Start date of the audit

kpis.name: Automated Inventory Ratio
kpis.description: The percentage of inventory management tasks that are automated, enhancing efficiency and reducing manual errors.
kpis.formula: (Number of Automated Inventory Tasks / Total Inventory Tasks) x 100
kpis.example: If 30 out of 50 inventory tasks are automated, the ratio is 60%.

kpis.name: Average Affiliate Earnings Per Click
kpis.description: Average earnings generated from a single click on an affiliate's promotional link. A key metric for affiliates to gauge the potential profitability of promoting a product.
kpis.formula: Total earnings from affiliate links / Total number of clicks on those links
kpis.example: If an affiliate earns $200 from 500 clicks, the EPC is $0.40.

kpis.name: Average After Call Work Time
kpis.description: The average time an agent spends on tasks related to the call (e.g., data entry, follow-up tasks) after the call ends. After Call Work Time can indicate the complexity or nature of the issues being addressed. If it's high, it might mean that calls involve complex issues or that agents need better tools to reduce post-call tasks. Efficient post-call processes can boost agent availability and overall productivity.
kpis.formula: Total after-call work time / Number of handled calls
kpis.example: If agents spent a combined total of 250 minutes on after-call work for 100 calls, the Average After Call Work Time would be 2.5 minutes.

kpis.name: Average Age of Inventory
kpis.description: Represents the average number of days items stay in inventory before being sold. Shorter ages mean faster turnover, which is often preferable.
kpis.formula: 365 / Inventory Turnover
kpis.example: If inventory turns over 5 times a year, the average age is 73 days.

kpis.name: Average Backlinks per Post
kpis.description: The average number of external websites linking to a blog post. Backlinks improve search engine rankings and indicate the content's authority and value.
kpis.formula: Total backlinks for all posts / Number of relevant posts
kpis.example: If 10 blog posts have 50 backlinks in total, the average backlinks per post is 5.

kpis.name: Average Bid Decision Time
kpis.description: Measures the average time taken for a decision to be made on a bid, critical for managing the sales cycle and resource allocation.
kpis.formula: (Sum of Decision Times for All Bids / Number of Bids)
kpis.example: If the total decision time for 5 bids is 150 days, the average bid decision time is 150/5 = 30 days.

kpis.name: Average Booth Engagement Time
kpis.description: The average time attendees spend engaged with your booth, presentation, or session. Indicates the relevance and appeal of your presentation or materials.

kpis.name: Average Call Handling Time
kpis.description: The average duration taken by an agent to handle a call from start to finish. A shorter handling time can indicate efficient and effective agents. However, if it's too short, it may mean issues are not being thoroughly addressed, potentially leading to repeat calls or unsatisfied customers.
kpis.formula: Total duration of all calls / Number of calls handled
kpis.example: If in one day, a call center handled 100 calls with a total duration of 500 minutes, the Average Call Handling Time would be 5 minutes.

kpis.name: Average Chat Duration
kpis.description: The average time taken to complete a chat session with a customer, in minutes. This metric helps understand the efficiency and effectiveness of chat interactions. Long durations might indicate complex issues or inefficiencies, while very short chats could suggest unsatisfactory or incomplete resolutions.
kpis.formula: Total chat duration time / Number of chat sessions
kpis.example: If the total time of 20 chat sessions was 400 minutes, the Average Chat Duration is 20 minutes.

kpis.name: Average Comments per Post
kpis.description: The average number of comments received per blog post. Comments reflect reader engagement, feedback, and community building around the content.
kpis.formula: Total comments for all posts / Number of posts
kpis.example: If a blog with 20 posts receives 200 comments in total, the average comments per post is 10.

kpis.name: Average Cost of Episode Promotion
kpis.description: The average monetary expense incurred in promoting each episode. Helps in measuring the ROI of your podcast promotion.

kpis.name: Average Cost of Training per Employee
kpis.description: Indicates the average amount spent by the organization on training each employee. Training is crucial for employee development and maintaining a competitive edge. By understanding this cost, companies can assess the value they place on training and compare against industry standards or past performance.
kpis.formula: Total Training Costs / Total Number of Employees Trained
kpis.example: If total training costs amount to $50,000 and 100 employees are trained, the Average Cost of Training per Employee is $500.

kpis.name: Average Cost per Call
kpis.description: The average cost incurred by the call center for handling a single call. This metric helps in understanding the operational efficiency of the call center. A high cost per call might indicate inefficiencies, higher overhead, or the need for better training, while a lower cost can suggest effective operations.
kpis.formula: Total operational costs / Total number of handled calls
kpis.example: If a call center's operational costs for a day are $1000 and they handle 200 calls, the Average Cost per Call is $5.

kpis.name: Average Cost to Recruit per Job Position
kpis.description:
Quantifies the expenses incurred in the recruitment process for a specific role, taking into account advertisements, recruiter fees, interview costs, and more.

It's vital for budgeting and determining the ROI of various recruitment strategies. If costs are too high for a particular role, it might necessitate a strategy shift.
kpis.formula: (Total recruitment costs for a position / Number of hires for that position)
kpis.example: An average expense of $5,000 to recruit for a managerial role.

kpis.name: Average Days of Vacancy Duration by Function
kpis.description:
Indicates the average number of days specific job roles or functions remain open before being filled. It provides a more granular look into the hiring process, targeting specific departments or roles.

Some roles might be harder to fill due to skill scarcity, location challenges, or other factors. Identifying such roles allows HR to tailor their approach, perhaps seeking passive candidates or enhancing the perks for those roles.
kpis.formula: (Total days vacancies were open for a specific function / Number of vacancies for that function)
kpis.example: Engineering roles vacant for an average of 40 days suggests challenges in sourcing tech talent.

kpis.name: Average Deal Lifespan
kpis.description: Average time taken for a deal to move from the initial stage to closing, key for predicting sales cycles and cash flow timing.
kpis.formula: Sum of all deal lifespans / Total number of deals
kpis.example: If 10 deals take an average of 60 days each to close, the average lifespan is 60 days.

kpis.name: Average Deal Size
kpis.description: The average revenue expected per sale, which helps in revenue forecasting and resource allocation.
kpis.formula: Total Pipeline Value / Number of Deals
kpis.example: If the pipeline value is $50,000 spread over 10 deals, the average deal size is $50,000/10 = $5,000.

kpis.name: Average Dock-to-Stock Time
kpis.description: This metric indicates the average time it takes for goods to move from the receiving dock to available inventory. Efficient processes reduce this time, making inventory available quicker.
kpis.formula: Total Time Taken from Dock to Stock for All Goods / Number of Goods Processed
kpis.example: If it took a total of 100 hours to process 50 goods, the average dock-to-stock time is 2 hours.

kpis.name: Average Employee Salary
kpis.description: The mean salary paid to employees. Helps in benchmarking compensation against industry standards, ensuring that the organization remains competitive in attracting and retaining talent.
kpis.formula: (Total Salary Expense / Number of Employees)
kpis.example: If the total salary expense for 50 employees in a group is $2,500,000 annually, the average employee salary would be $50,000.

kpis.name: Average Hourly Fee
kpis.description: The mean amount charged to clients per consultant hour. Provides insights into the pricing structure and competitive positioning.
kpis.formula: Total Revenue from Hourly Projects / Total Hours Billed
kpis.example: If a consultant bills 100 hours at a total of $10,000, the Average Hourly Fee would be $100.

kpis.name: Average Inventory Value
kpis.description: This indicates the average monetary value of inventory held over a period. Tracking this KPI can help in understanding capital tied up in inventory and the need for effective inventory management.
kpis.formula: (Beginning Inventory Value + Ending Inventory Value) / 2
kpis.example: If beginning inventory is $100,000 and ending inventory is $150,000, the average inventory value is $125,000.

kpis.name: Average Order Value from Affiliate Sales
kpis.description: The average monetary value of orders placed through affiliate links. Provides insight into the spending behavior of customers acquired through affiliates.
kpis.formula: Total revenue from affiliate sales / Number of orders from affiliate sales
kpis.example: If affiliate sales totaled $5,000 from 100 orders, the AOV is $50.

kpis.name: Average Overtime Hours per Employee
kpis.description:
The average number of overtime hours worked by an employee within a specified period, commonly monthly or annually.

Frequent and prolonged overtime might indicate issues such as understaffing, unrealistic expectations, or inefficient workflows. While occasional overtime might be necessary, consistently high levels can lead to burnout and decreased employee well-being.
kpis.formula: Total number of overtime hours worked by all employees / Total number of employees
kpis.example: In a month, if employees in a department of 50 people clock in 200 overtime hours in total, the average overtime per employee is 4 hours.

kpis.name: Average Paid Time Off
kpis.description:
The average number of paid days off (like vacations, personal days) that an employee takes during a specified timeframe, usually annually.

Monitoring this KPI can help companies ensure work-life balance for their employees, prevent burnout, and maintain productivity levels. High or low averages might indicate cultural or policy issues that need addressing.
kpis.formula: Total number of paid days off taken by all employees / Total number of employees
kpis.example: If in a company of 100 employees, 1200 days off are taken collectively during a year, the average is 12 days per employee.

kpis.name: Average Payment Cycle Time
kpis.description: The average time it takes for the company to process and pay an invoice upon its receipt, in days. A shorter cycle time can lead to better relationships with suppliers and potential early-payment discounts.
kpis.formula: Total days taken to pay all invoices / Number of invoices
kpis.example: If it took 150 days to pay 10 invoices, the average payment cycle time is 15 days.

kpis.name: Average Percentage Viewed
kpis.description: Reflects viewer engagement by showing the average percentage of the video that viewers watched. A lower percentage might indicate that viewers lost interest and dropped off early.
kpis.formula: (Total Watch Time / (Number of Plays x Video Length)) x 100
kpis.example: If a 10-minute video has a total watch time of 500 minutes and was played 100 times, the average percentage viewed is 50%. You can also calculate this as an aggregate of all videos in a channel.

kpis.name: Average Post-Training Quiz Score
kpis.description: Indicates the average score achieved by employees in quizzes or tests taken after their compliance training sessions. Scores can reflect the effectiveness of the training and the comprehension of employees.
kpis.formula: Total score of all quizzes / Number of quizzes taken
kpis.example: If the total score from 100 quizzes is 8,500, the average score is 85.

kpis.name: Average Profit Margin per Sale
kpis.description: This KPI measures the average profit made per sale, indicating pricing strategies' effectiveness and market positioning.
kpis.formula: (Total Profit / Number of Sales) * 100
kpis.example: If a company's total profit is $20,000 from 400 sales, the average margin per sale is (20,000/400)*100 = 5%.

kpis.name: Average Project Duration
kpis.description: The mean time taken to complete projects, calculated over a specified period. By understanding the average time projects take, consultancies can better manage client expectations, allocate resources, and identify areas of inefficiency.
kpis.formula: Total duration of all projects / Number of projects in a given time frame
kpis.example: If a consultancy completed 5 projects in January with durations of 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 days, the Average Project Duration for January would be 20 days.

kpis.name: Average Resolution Time
kpis.description: The average time taken to resolve a customer's issue from the moment it was raised. A shorter resolution time indicates efficient processes and can increase customer satisfaction.
kpis.formula: Total time taken to resolve issues / Total number of resolved issues
kpis.example: If 50 issues took a combined total of 100 hours to resolve, the average resolution time is 2 hours.

kpis.name: Average Resolution Time
kpis.description: The average time taken to resolve a customer's issue from the moment it was raised. A shorter resolution time indicates efficient processes and can increase customer satisfaction.
kpis.formula: Total time taken for resolutions / Total number of resolutions
kpis.example: If 50 issues took a combined total of 100 hours to resolve, the average resolution time is 2 hours.

kpis.name: Average Response Time for Routine HR Inquiries
kpis.description: A shorter response time can lead to higher employee satisfaction and trust in the HR department. Monitoring this KPI can highlight areas where the HR team might need additional resources or training.
kpis.formula: Total Time Taken to Respond to All Inquiries / Total Number of Inquiries
kpis.example: If it took 50 hours in total to respond to 25 inquiries, the Average Response Time would be 2 hours.

kpis.name: Average Revenue Per Customer
kpis.description: Average Revenue Per Customer (ARPC), also known as Average Revenue Per User (ARPU) in some contexts, is a measure of the revenue generated per user or unit of customer, usually calculated over a specific time period (such as monthly or annually). This KPI is important because it helps businesses understand their revenue patterns and can guide decisions around pricing, customer segmentation, and sales and marketing strategy.
kpis.formula: Total Revenue / Total Number of Customers
kpis.example: If a company generates $1,000,000 in total revenue from 500 customers, the ARPC is $2,000. This means the company earns, on average, $2,000 per customer.

kpis.name: Average Sales Rep Onboarding Time
kpis.description: Measures the average time taken for a new sales representative to go through the onboarding process, which includes training and reaching full productivity.
kpis.formula: (Total Onboarding Time for All New Hires / Number of New Hires)
kpis.example: If the total onboarding time for 4 new hires is 120 days, the average is (120/4) = 30 days per new hire.

kpis.name: Average Sales Training Costs per Employee
kpis.description: Tracks the average cost spent on training for each sales representative, indicating the investment in the team’s development.
kpis.formula: (Total Training Costs / Number of Sales Representatives)
kpis.example: If $5,000 is spent on training 10 sales reps, the average cost per rep is $5,000/10 = $500.

kpis.name: Average Screens Per Visit
kpis.description: Measures the average number of screens or pages users navigate through in a single app session. It's indicative of user engagement and interest in exploring the app's content and features. Higher numbers suggest deeper engagement.
kpis.formula: Average Number of Screens Viewed Per Session
kpis.example: If users view an average of 6 screens per visit, it suggests a high level of engagement with the app.

kpis.name: Average Session Duration
kpis.description: The average amount of time a user spends on your site during a single session. Longer session durations can indicate more engaging content or effective user experience.
kpis.formula: Total duration of all sessions (in seconds) / number of sessions
kpis.example: If users spent a total of 5,000 minutes on your site over 1,000 sessions, the average session duration is 5 minutes.

kpis.name: Average Sick Days Per Employee
kpis.description: Reflects the average number of sick days taken by each employee annually. Monitoring this can provide early warnings about workplace health issues or stress, impacting productivity and well-being.
kpis.formula: Total Sick Days Taken / Total Number of Employees
kpis.example: If employees took 500 sick days in total and there are 100 employees, the average is 5 days per employee.

kpis.name: Average Sourcing Cost per Hire
kpis.description: Measures the average cost associated with sourcing (finding and attracting) each new hire. Recruiting costs can vary based on the methods used. This metric helps organizations understand which methods are most cost-effective and how much they are investing to attract talent.
kpis.formula: Total Sourcing Costs / Total Number of Hires
kpis.example: If total sourcing costs for a year are $40,000 and the company hired 80 new employees, the Average Sourcing Cost per Hire would be $500.

kpis.name: Average Time from Job Acceptance until Job Start
kpis.description: Captures the duration between a candidate accepting a job offer and their official start date. A prolonged gap might reduce the enthusiasm of a new hire or provide them with opportunities to consider other offers. Quick onboarding ensures that the momentum from the hiring process carries into the employee's tenure.
kpis.formula: (Total days from offer acceptance to start date / Number of accepted offers)
kpis.example: A 10-day gap between acceptance and joining suggests a swift onboarding process.

kpis.name: Average Time on Market
kpis.description: Determines the average time a product takes from being listed for sale to being sold, indicating market demand.
kpis.formula: Total Days on Market / Number of Units Sold
kpis.example: If 100 units sold in 200 days, average time is 200 days / 100 units = 2 days per unit.

kpis.name: Average Time to Conversion
kpis.description: The average time taken for a prospect to become a customer from the initial touchpoint. Gives an idea of the sales cycle duration.
kpis.formula: Total time taken for all conversions / Number of conversions
kpis.example: If 10 customers took a total of 50 days to convert, the average time is 5 days.

kpis.name: Average Time to Do One Audit
kpis.description: Measures the average duration taken to complete a single audit. Prolonged audit durations might indicate inefficiencies in the audit process or complex compliance issues that need addressing.
kpis.formula: Total time taken for all audits in a period / Number of audits in that period
kpis.example: If 10 audits took a total of 200 days, the average time per audit is 20 days.

kpis.name: Average Vacation Days Per Employee
kpis.description: Shows the average number of vacation days taken by each employee annually. Low utilization may signal overwork or a culture where employees feel they can't take time off. High utilization without corresponding productivity might be a concern.
kpis.formula: Total Vacation Days Taken / Total Number of Employees
kpis.example: If employees took 1,000 vacation days in total and there are 100 employees, the average is 10 days per employee.

kpis.name: Average View Duration
kpis.description: The average length of time viewers spend watching the video. Provides insight into viewer engagement and interest levels.

kpis.name: Average Views per Post
kpis.description: The average number of views a blog post receives. Helps in understanding which topics or content types resonate most with the audience.
kpis.formula: Total views for all posts / Number of posts
kpis.example: If a blog with 10 posts receives 10,000 views in total, the average views per post is 1,000.

kpis.name: Average Wait Time Before Connecting To An Agent
kpis.description: The average duration a customer waits in a queue before being connected to a service agent. Longer wait times can lead to customer frustration and negatively impact overall satisfaction.
kpis.formula: Total wait time for all customers / Total number of customers
kpis.example: If 200 customers waited a combined total of 400 minutes, the average wait time is 2 minutes.

kpis.name: Backlinks
kpis.description: Refers to the total number of external links from other websites pointing to your website. Backlinks are a significant factor in search engine rankings. Quality and relevance of the backlinking sites are crucial. Besides the number of links, you may also look into total number of referring domains, links lost, and links earned.
kpis.formula: Total count of incoming links from external websites.
kpis.example: If 200 different websites link to your content, you have 200 backlinks.

kpis.name: Backorder Rate
kpis.description: The ratio of items that are ordered but currently out of stock to total sales, indicating how often sales cannot be immediately fulfilled.
kpis.formula: (Number of Items on Backorder / Total Sales) x 100
kpis.example: If 20 items are on backorder out of 200 sold, the backorder rate is 10%.

kpis.name: Bad Debt to Sales Ratio
kpis.description: This metric gives the percentage of sales that have been written off as uncollectible. It provides insights into the effectiveness of credit policies and collection efforts. A higher ratio could indicate lenient credit policies or an ineffective collections department.
kpis.formula: (Bad Debts / Total Sales) x 100
kpis.example: If bad debts are $1,000 and total sales are $100,000, then Bad Debt to Sales Ratio = (1,000 / 100,000) x 100 = 1%.

kpis.name: Batch Size
kpis.description: Batch Size pertains to the number of tasks or deliverables processed as a group. Adjusting batch size can improve efficiency, reduce wait times, and optimize resource usage.
kpis.formula: Batch Size = Number of tasks/deliverables processed together
kpis.example: If 5 tasks are grouped and processed collectively, the batch size is 5.

kpis.name: Benefit Utilization Rate
kpis.description: Quantifies the percentage of employees making use of a specific benefit provided by the company. Offering benefits is only effective if employees utilize them. Monitoring the utilization rate helps companies adjust their benefits package, ensuring they invest in perks that are meaningful to employees.
kpis.formula: (Number of employees using a particular benefit / Total number of eligible employees) x 100
kpis.example: If a company offers gym memberships to 100 employees, and 70 of them sign up, the utilization rate is 70%.

kpis.name: Benefits as a Percentage of Salary
kpis.description: The proportion of total benefits expense to the total salary expense. This metric provides insight into the company's compensation structure, helping to assess the attractiveness of its total compensation package. A high percentage indicates a generous benefits package.
kpis.formula: (Total Benefits Expenditure / Total Salary Expenditure) x 100
kpis.example: If the total benefits expense is $250,000 and the total salary expense is $2,500,000, the percentage would be 10%.

kpis.name: Bid Conversion Rate
kpis.description: Tracks the rate at which bids are converted into contracts, indicative of the effectiveness of the bidding proposals and negotiation skills.
kpis.formula: (Number of Bids Converted to Contracts / Total Bids Submitted) * 100
kpis.example: If 5 out of 50 submitted bids are converted to contracts, the conversion rate is (5/50)*100 = 10%.

kpis.name: Bid Quality Score
kpis.description: Uses a scoring system to evaluate the quality of bids based on predefined criteria, reflecting the likelihood of winning and alignment with client needs.
kpis.formula: (Average of Assigned Scores per Bid)
kpis.example: If 5 bids receive scores of 6, 7, 8, 7, and 6, the bid quality score is (6+7+8+7+6)/5 = 6.8.

kpis.name: Bid Success Rate
kpis.description: Reflects the effectiveness of the bid strategy by measuring the proportion of successful bids out of the total submitted, indicating competitiveness and proposal quality.
kpis.formula: (Number of Successful Bids / Total Bids Submitted) * 100
kpis.example: If a company wins 20 out of 100 bids, the success rate is (20/100)*100 = 20%.

kpis.name: Bid-to-Win Ratio
kpis.description: Assesses the efficiency of the bidding process by comparing the number of bids made to the number of wins, used to gauge the effectiveness of bid qualification processes.
kpis.formula: (Number of Wins / Number of Bids Made)
kpis.example: If a company makes 50 bids and wins 10, the bid-to-win ratio is 10/50 = 0.2.

kpis.name: Blog Conversion Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of blog visitors who take a desired action, such as subscribing, downloading a content offer, or making a purchase. Indicates the effectiveness of the blog in driving desired actions and its contribution to business goals.
kpis.formula: (Number of conversions from the blog / Total blog visitors) x 100
kpis.example:
If 100 out of 2,000 blog visitors subscribe, the blog conversion rate is 5%.

The metric can also be applied locally to a specific campaign. For example, if a blog is meant to promote an ebook, conversion rate for that particular asset can be calculated based on the number of views recorded for the posts that mention the ebook.

kpis.name: Blog Subscribers
kpis.description: The total number of individuals subscribed to receive updates from the blog, either via email or RSS feeds. A growing subscriber base indicates consistent value provision and reader loyalty. Subscribers are more likely to engage and convert.
kpis.formula: Total number of email + RSS subscribers
kpis.example: If a blog has 1,500 email subscribers and 200 RSS subscribers, the total is 1,700 blog subscribers.

kpis.name: Bounce Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of sent emails that couldn't be delivered. High bounce rates, for example above 5%, can affect your email sender reputation.
kpis.formula: (Number of Undelivered Emails / Number of Emails Sent) x 100
kpis.example: If 1,000 emails are sent and 50 bounce back, the bounce rate is 5%.

kpis.name: Bounce Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of visitors who navigate away from the site after viewing only one page. A high bounce rate might indicate that visitors aren't finding what they're looking for or that user experience is lacking.
kpis.formula: (Single-page sessions / Total sessions) x 100
kpis.example: If 500 out of 1,000 sessions were single-page visits, the bounce rate is 50%.

kpis.name: Brand Awareness
kpis.description: Measures the percentage of target market familiar with a brand. Higher brand awareness means more people recognize and recall your brand, potentially leading to increased sales and market share.
kpis.formula: Higher brand awareness means more people recognize and recall your brand, potentially leading to increased sales and market share.
kpis.example:
If 500 out of a 1000 surveyed individuals know a brand, the brand awareness is 50%.

To find out if respondents are familiar with a brand, respondents are typically given a list of brands and asked which ones they recognize. This method checks if they can recognize your brand among others. For example, "Which of the following brands are you familiar with?" followed by multiple brand options, including the brand you're measuring.

In an alternative approach, respondents are asked to list brands in a particular category without any prompts. This checks which brands come to their mind spontaneously.

For example, "Can you name any smartphone brands you know?" If your brand is Apple, you'd be looking for it in their answers.

kpis.name: Brand Consistency
kpis.description: Measures how uniformly a brand is presented across all channels and touchpoints. Consistent branding fosters trust and makes the brand recognizable.
kpis.formula: (Number of consistent brand touchpoints / Total brand touchpoints) x 100
kpis.example: If 9 out of 10 advertising campaigns follow the brand guidelines, brand consistency is 90%.

kpis.name: Brand Loyalty Rate
kpis.description: Evaluates the portion of customers who make repeat purchases or show a strong preference for your brand over competitors. Loyal customers are less likely to switch to competitors, more likely to recommend your brand, and tend to spend more.
kpis.formula: (Number of repeat customers / Total number of customers) x 100
kpis.example: If you have 1000 customers and 700 of them make repeat purchases, your Brand Loyalty Rate is 70%.

kpis.name: Brand Mentions During Event
kpis.description: The number of times your brand or company is mentioned during presentations, in discussions, or on social media related to the event. Indicates the level of brand awareness and buzz generated from the event.

kpis.name: Brand Recall
kpis.description: The ability of consumers to recognize and remember a brand without any prompts. Effective advertising and branding campaigns should lead to better recall, influencing purchase decisions.
kpis.formula: (Number of people who recall the brand unprompted / Total surveyed) x 100
kpis.example:
If 400 out of 1000 surveyed individuals recall a brand without prompting, the brand recall is 40%.

To measure how many respondents can remember a brand without any prompting after being exposed to it, after exposing the respondents to a brand (e.g., showing a commercial, advertisement, or discussing the product category), you'd wait for a specific period. Then, you'd ask them about the brands they remember from the exposure without giving any hints or options.

kpis.name: Brand Reputation Score
kpis.description: Measures the perception and sentiment towards a brand in the market. A positive reputation enhances customer trust, loyalty, and can lead to increased sales.
kpis.formula: (Total positive mentions - Total negative mentions) / Total mentions
kpis.example:
If a brand has 70 positive mentions, 20 negative mentions out of 100, the score is 0.5.

Here, a score of 0.5 (or 50%) means there are more positive mentions than negative. If the score was 0, it would mean an equal number of positive and negative mentions. If negative, it indicates more negative mentions than positive.

kpis.name: Break-even Analysis
kpis.description: Determines when total revenue will equal total costs, signifying the point at which profit begins.
kpis.formula: Fixed Costs / (Selling Price per Unit - Variable Cost per Unit)
kpis.example: For $10,000 in fixed costs, a selling price of $100, and variable cost of $60, break-even is $10,000 / ($100 - $60) = 250 units.

kpis.name: Budget Variance
kpis.description: The difference between the budgeted amount for any category and the actual amount spent. Allows companies to track their financial performance and make necessary adjustments if they're overspending or underspending.
kpis.formula: Budgeted Amount - Actual Amount Spent
kpis.example: If the budget for marketing was $1 million and the actual spent was $800,000, the budget variance would be $200,000.

kpis.name: Bug Density
kpis.description: Indicates the number of bugs per a certain amount of lines of code, providing insight into the overall quality of the code.
kpis.formula: (Number of Bugs / Lines of Code)
kpis.example: For 50 bugs in 10,000 lines of code, the bug density is 50/10,000.

kpis.name: Build Failure Rate
kpis.description: Calculates the frequency of build failures in the Continuous Integration (CI) process. Frequent build failures can disrupt the development workflow and delay releases.
kpis.formula: (Number of Failed Builds / Total Builds) * 100
kpis.example: If 10 out of 200 builds fail, the build failure rate is (10/200)*100 = 5%.

kpis.name: Burn Rate
kpis.description: The rate at which a company is spending its capital, especially important for startups or growth-focused companies. It helps investors and company executives determine how long the company can operate before needing additional funding.
kpis.formula: Total Cash Spent Over a Period / Number of Months in that Period
kpis.example: If a startup spent $300,000 over 3 months, its burn rate is $100,000/month.

kpis.name: Call Abandonment Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of incoming calls that are abandoned by the caller before connecting to an agent. A high abandonment rate could indicate long wait times, technical issues, or a misalignment in customer expectations. Monitoring this can help in improving customer experience and ensuring potential customers aren't lost.
kpis.formula: (Number of abandoned calls / Total number of incoming calls) x 100
kpis.example: If there were 20 abandoned calls out of 200 incoming calls, the Call Abandonment Rate would be 10%.

kpis.name: Call Resolution Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of calls where the customer's issue is resolved in a single interaction. This metric directly correlates with customer satisfaction. A higher resolution rate indicates that customer issues are being addressed effectively without the need for multiple callbacks, which can increase trust and loyalty.
kpis.formula: (Number of calls with issues resolved / Total number of handled calls) x 100
kpis.example: If out of 100 handled calls, 85 had their issues resolved, the Call Resolution Rate would be 85%.

kpis.name: Call Service Level
kpis.description: The percentage of calls answered within a predefined threshold time. Meeting service levels ensures that callers aren't waiting excessively. High service levels typically correlate with better customer satisfaction and indicate efficient staffing and operations.
kpis.formula: (Number of calls answered within threshold / Total number of incoming calls) x 100
kpis.example: If the goal is to answer all calls within 20 seconds, and 80 out of 100 incoming calls are answered within that threshold, the Call Service Level would be 80%.

kpis.name: Call Transfer Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of calls that are transferred to another agent or department. A high transfer rate might indicate that customers are not being routed to the best agent or department initially, which can lead to longer wait times and potential frustration. Ensuring customers reach the right person the first time increases satisfaction and reduces handling time.
kpis.formula: (Number of transferred calls / Total number of handled calls) x 100
kpis.example: If out of 100 handled calls, 10 were transferred, the Call Transfer Rate would be 10%.

kpis.name: Call Volume
kpis.description: The total number of incoming calls to the call center within a given time frame. Understanding call volume helps in planning resource allocation, such as staffing. A sudden increase in call volume might indicate a surge in interest or a potential issue that many customers are facing.

kpis.name: Capacity Utilization
kpis.description: Measures how effectively the development team utilizes their available capacity for deployments and handling changes. It helps in assessing the workload balance and resource allocation efficiency.
kpis.formula: (Used Capacity / Total Available Capacity) * 100
kpis.example: If a team operates at 800 hours out of a 1,000-hour capacity, the utilization rate is (800/1000)*100 = 80%.

kpis.name: Carrying Costs, Finished Goods
kpis.description: These are the costs associated with holding inventory that's ready for sale. High carrying costs may indicate inefficiencies in sales or over-purchasing.
kpis.formula: Total Costs Associated with Holding Finished Goods Inventory
kpis.example: If the total expenses related to holding finished goods for a month are $5,000, the carrying costs are $5,000.

kpis.name: Change Failure Rate
kpis.description: Calculates the proportion of deployments that result in failure in production, necessitating immediate remedies like hotfixes or rollbacks. Unlike general failure rates, it specifically focuses on failures linked to recent changes, excluding issues like spontaneous outages or user-end errors.
kpis.formula: (Number of Failed Deployments / Total Deployments) * 100
kpis.example: If there are 5 failures out of 100 deployments, the change failure rate is (5/100)*100 = 5%.

kpis.name: Change Success Rate
kpis.description: Assesses the percentage of changes or deployments that are successfully implemented without causing failures or outages. It's an important indicator of the deployment quality and the effectiveness of the change management process.
kpis.formula: (Successful Changes / Total Changes) * 100
kpis.example: If 120 out of 150 changes are successful, the change success rate is (120/150)*100 = 80%.

kpis.name: Change Success Rate
kpis.description: Measures the percentage of changes applied to the system that are successful without causing incidents or degradations, indicating the effectiveness of change management.
kpis.formula: (Successful Changes / Total Changes) * 100
kpis.example: If out of 100 changes, 95 are successful, the success rate is (95/100)*100 = 95%.

kpis.name: Chat Abandonment Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of chat sessions initiated by customers that are ended before a resolution. High abandonment rates can indicate customer frustration, long wait times, or unhelpful support.
kpis.formula: (Number of abandoned chats / Total initiated chats) x 100
kpis.example: If 15 out of 100 chats were abandoned by customers, the Chat Abandonment Rate is 15%.

kpis.name: Chat Completion Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of chat sessions that reach a resolution compared to those initiated. This KPI ensures that initiated chats lead to conclusive support and satisfactory outcomes, enhancing customer experience.
kpis.formula: (Number of completed chats / Total initiated chats) x 100
kpis.example: If 90 out of 100 initiated chats reached a resolution, the Chat Completion Rate is 90%.

kpis.name: Chat Engagement Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of website visitors or app users who engage in a chat session. Engagement rates can provide insights into how proactive or appealing your chat interface is and if users feel the need to ask questions or get clarifications.
kpis.formula: (Number of chat sessions / Total website or app visitors) x 100
kpis.example: If 50 out of 1000 website visitors initiated a chat, the Chat Engagement Rate is 5%.

kpis.name: Chat Resolution Time
kpis.description: The average time taken to resolve a customer's issue or query during a chat session, in minutes. Shorter resolution times often lead to higher customer satisfaction. Monitoring this KPI ensures efficiency and effectiveness in the chat support process.
kpis.formula: Total resolution time for chats / Number of resolved chat cases
kpis.example: If it took 300 minutes in total to resolve 30 chat cases, the Chat Resolution Time is 10 minutes.

kpis.name: Chat Transfers
kpis.description: The number of times a chat session is transferred from one agent to another. Frequent transfers can be a sign of inefficiencies, lack of expertise, or inadequate training. They can also frustrate customers due to repetition and prolonged resolution times.
kpis.formula: Total number of chat transfers
kpis.example: If 10 chats were transferred to different agents in a day, the Chat Transfers count for that day is 10.

kpis.name: Chat Volume
kpis.description: The total number of chat sessions initiated within a given time frame. Like Email Volume, monitoring chat volume provides insights into customer inquiries, resource allocation, and potential product or service issues.
kpis.formula: Total number of chat sessions
kpis.example: If 200 chat sessions were initiated on a particular day, the Chat Volume for that day is 200.

kpis.name: Chatbot Resolution Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of chat sessions handled and resolved solely by chatbots without human intervention. A high resolution rate by chatbots can indicate the effectiveness of AI in handling common queries, leading to resource savings and faster response times.
kpis.formula: (Number of chats resolved by chatbots / Total chats handled by chatbots) x 100
kpis.example: If a chatbot resolved 50 out of 60 sessions it handled, the Chatbot Resolution Rate is 83.3%.

kpis.name: Churn Rate of Affiliates
kpis.description: Percentage of affiliates or partners who stop promoting your product or service. A high churn rate can indicate dissatisfaction or inefficiencies in the affiliate program.
kpis.formula: (Number of affiliates who left / Total number of affiliates at the start of the period) x 100
kpis.example: If 10 out of 150 affiliates stopped promoting in a month, the churn rate is 6.67%.

kpis.name: Click-Through Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of impressions that resulted in a click. A higher Click-Through Rate (CTR) suggests that the ad is relevant and compelling to the audience.
kpis.formula: (Total clicks / Total impressions) x 100
kpis.example: If an ad receives 100 clicks from 1,000 impressions, the CTR is 10%.

kpis.name: Click-to-Open Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of recipients who clicked on a link in the email out of those who opened the email. Gauges the effectiveness of the email content among engaged recipients.
kpis.formula: (Number of Clicks / Number of Emails Opened) x 100
kpis.example: If 200 people open an email and 40 click a link, the click-to-open rate is 20%.

kpis.name: Client Concentration Risk
kpis.description: Measures the dependency of the consultancy on its largest clients. It provides a view of how much of the firm's revenue is generated by a small number of top clients. High client concentration can pose a significant risk. If a major client decides to leave or faces financial troubles, it could have a substantial negative impact on the consultancy's revenue.
kpis.formula: (Total revenue from top 'X' clients / Total revenue) * 100
kpis.example: If a consultancy's top 3 clients generate $500,000 out of a total revenue of $1 million, the Client Concentration Risk is 50%.

kpis.name: Client Referral Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of new clients acquired through existing client referrals. Referrals are a strong indicator of client satisfaction and can be a cost-effective way of acquiring new business.
kpis.formula: (Number of clients through referrals / Total new clients) x 100
kpis.example: If 15 out of 50 new clients were referred by existing clients, the referral rate is 30%.

kpis.name: Client Retention Rate
kpis.description: The proportion of clients who continue to engage with your consultancy over a specific time period. High retention rates indicate client satisfaction and can be more cost-effective than acquiring new clients.
kpis.formula: [(Clients at End of Period - New Clients) / Clients at Start of Period] x 100
kpis.example: If you started the year with 100 clients, gained 20 new ones, but ended the year with 110, the retention rate would be 90%.

kpis.name: Client Retention Rate
kpis.description: Reflects the percentage of clients retained over a specific period, indicating account stability and customer satisfaction.
kpis.formula: (Number of Customers at End of Period - Number of New Customers) / Number of Customers at Start of Period * 100
kpis.example: If there were 100 customers at the start, 10 new customers, and 90 remaining, the rate is (90 - 10) / 100 * 100 = 80%.

kpis.name: Client Satisfaction Score
kpis.description: An aggregate score derived from feedback and ratings given by clients post-delivery or at project milestones. It offers direct insight into how clients perceive the value and quality of the consultancy's services.
kpis.formula: Sum of all client scores / Number of clients giving feedback
kpis.example: If 5 clients rated your service as 4, 5, 3, 4, and 5 stars, the client satisfaction score would be 4.2 stars.

kpis.name: Code Complexity
kpis.description: Measures the complexity of the code, which can impact maintainability and readability. High complexity often correlates with increased likelihood of errors and difficulty in understanding the code.
kpis.formula: Calculated using various algorithms like Cyclomatic Complexity
kpis.example: If a module has a cyclomatic complexity of 10, it's considered more complex and potentially harder to maintain than one with a complexity of 5.

kpis.name: Code Coverage
kpis.description: Represents the percentage of code that is covered by automated tests, which is crucial for ensuring that as much code as possible is tested to identify defects.
kpis.formula: (Number of Lines of Code Tested / Total Lines of Code) * 100
kpis.example: If 800 out of 1,000 lines of code are tested, the code coverage is (800/1000)*100 = 80%.

kpis.name: Code Duplication
kpis.description: Quantifies the amount of duplicated code in a codebase. High duplication can lead to larger codebases, making maintenance more challenging and increasing the potential for errors.
kpis.formula: (Duplicated Lines of Code / Total Lines of Code) * 100
kpis.example: If a project has 1,000 lines of code with 100 lines duplicated, the code duplication rate is (100/1000)*100 = 10%.

kpis.name: Code Smells
kpis.description: Indicators of deeper problems in code, 'code smells' are patterns that may not be outright bugs but suggest design issues that can increase the risk of bugs or failures in the future.
kpis.formula: Count of Identified Code Smells
kpis.example: If a code review identifies 15 code smells, the total count is 15.

kpis.name: Collections Effectiveness Index
kpis.description: Evaluates the effectiveness of a company's collections efforts over a specific period. Collections Effectiveness Index (CEI) indicates how well a company is converting its accounts receivables into cash. A CEI close to 100% indicates effective collections.
kpis.formula: (Beginning Receivables + Monthly Sales - Ending Total Receivables) / (All Beginning Receivables + Monthly Sales - Ending Current Receivables) x 100
kpis.example: If your company has a beginning A/R of $100,000, monthly sales of $200,000 and ending total A/R of $120,000, the collections effectiveness index would be calculated as: CEI = ($100,000 + 200,000 – 120,000) ÷ (100,000 + 200.000 – 120,000) x 100 = 100%

kpis.name: Complaint Resolution Satisfaction Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of customers who, after making a complaint, were satisfied with how the company addressed the issue. Resolving complaints in a satisfactory manner can convert a negative experience into a positive one, retaining customers and improving reputation.
kpis.formula: (Number of complainants satisfied with resolution / Total number of complainants) x 100
kpis.example: If 20 out of 30 complainants were satisfied with the resolution, the rate is 66.7%.

kpis.name: Complaints Received
kpis.description: The number of complaints lodged by customers within a given time frame. Monitoring complaints is crucial to identify recurring issues and improve service. A surge in complaints can signal bigger problems.
kpis.formula: Total number of customer complaints
kpis.example: If a company receives 20 complaints in a week, the Complaints Received count is 20 for that week.

kpis.name: Completion Call Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of calls that are successfully completed without being abandoned or disconnected. A high Completion Call Rate indicates the effectiveness of the call center's operations in keeping customers engaged and addressing their needs. Low rates can hint at dissatisfaction, technical issues, or premature ending of calls, which can affect customer trust.
kpis.formula: (Number of completed calls / Total number of incoming calls) x 100
kpis.example: Out of 300 incoming calls, if 270 were completed, the Completion Call Rate would be 90%.

kpis.name: Compliance Rate
kpis.description: Indicates how well the project's deliverables comply with relevant standards, regulations, or requirements. A high compliance rate reduces regulatory risks.
kpis.formula: (Number of compliant deliverables / Total deliverables) x 100
kpis.example: If 95 out of 100 deliverables meet the compliance standards, the compliance rate is 95%.

kpis.name: Compliments Received
kpis.description: The number of positive feedbacks or compliments received from customers about a service or product. Positive feedback can indicate successful initiatives, outstanding service, or product quality, and help motivate teams.
kpis.formula: Total number of compliments received
kpis.example: If a team received 40 compliments in a week, the Compliments Received count for that week is 40.

kpis.name: Concurrent Chats Per Agent
kpis.description: The average number of chat sessions an agent handles simultaneously. This metric gauges agent multitasking capabilities. Too many concurrent chats can decrease the quality of support, while too few might indicate underutilization.
kpis.formula: Total concurrent chats handled / Number of agents
kpis.example: If 5 agents handle a combined total of 25 chats simultaneously, Concurrent Chats Per Agent is 5.

kpis.name: Consultant Satisfaction Score
kpis.description: Captures the overall job satisfaction of consultants, typically gathered through regular surveys. Happy employees tend to be more productive, loyal, and offer better service to clients. Monitoring satisfaction can help in addressing potential issues before they escalate.
kpis.formula: Total stars or points given / Total number of consultants surveyed
kpis.example: If 50 consultants give a cumulative rating of 200 stars (in a 5-star system), the average consultant satisfaction score is 4 stars.

kpis.name: Consultant Training Hours
kpis.description: The total number of training hours dedicated to each consultant within a specified period, emphasizing the commitment to upskilling and continuous learning. A well-trained consultant can deliver better service, stay updated with industry trends, and improve client satisfaction. Investing in training can also lead to increased employee retention and job satisfaction.
kpis.formula: Total hours of training / Number of consultants
kpis.example: If a consultancy conducts 500 hours of training for 50 consultants, on average, each consultant receives 10 hours of training.

kpis.name: Consultant Turnover Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of employees or consultants who leave the consultancy within a specified period. High turnover rates can indicate dissatisfaction, poor company culture, or ineffective management. It can also lead to increased recruitment costs and potential knowledge loss.
kpis.formula: (Number of consultants who left / Average number of consultants during the period) x 100
kpis.example: If 5 out of 50 employees left in a year, the turnover rate is 10%.

kpis.name: Consultant Utilization Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of a consultant's working hours that are billable. It provides insights into how efficiently consultant hours are being utilized towards revenue-generating tasks.
kpis.formula: (Billable Hours / Total Available Working Hours) x 100
kpis.example: If a consultant has 40 available hours in a week and bills 32 hours, the utilization rate is 80%.

kpis.name: Content Usage by Sales
kpis.description: Measures the utilization rate of marketing content by the sales team during the sales process, indicative of the relevance and quality of content.
kpis.formula: (Content Pieces Used by Sales / Content Pieces Available)
kpis.example: If sales uses 30 out of 100 pieces of content, the content usage score is 30/100 = 0.3.

kpis.name: Contributing External Companies
kpis.description: Tracks the number of different companies or external organizations actively contributing to the project. It’s a measure of industry engagement and support for the project.
kpis.formula: Count of Contributing Companies
kpis.example: If 40 different companies contribute to a project, the total is 40.

kpis.name: Conversion Rate at Each Funnel Stage
kpis.description: The percentage of individuals who move to the next stage of the funnel. Helps identify bottlenecks or stages where prospects drop off.
kpis.formula: (Number of conversions / Number of leads at that stage) x 100
kpis.example: If 100 visitors visit a product page and 10 add the product to their cart, the conversion rate is 10%.

kpis.name: Conversion Rate from Free to Paid Users
kpis.description: This metric assesses the effectiveness of converting users from a free version of the software to a paid version. It’s a critical indicator of the software’s monetization success and perceived value among users. A high conversion rate suggests strong product satisfaction or successful upselling strategies, whereas a low rate might indicate a need to enhance perceived value or revise pricing structures.
kpis.formula: (Number of Users Converting from Free to Paid / Total Free Users) * 100
kpis.example: If 150 out of 2,000 free users upgrade to a paid version, the conversion rate is (150/2000)*100 = 7.5%.

kpis.name: Conversion Rate Post-Event
kpis.description: The percentage of leads from the event that convert into customers. Shows the quality of leads and the effectiveness of post-event follow-up.
kpis.formula: (Number of Conversions / Number of Leads) x 100
kpis.example: If 10 out of 50 leads from an event become customers, the conversion rate is 20%.

kpis.name: Conversions from PR Efforts
kpis.description: The number of desired actions (such as sign-ups, purchases, or downloads) resulting from PR-related content or campaigns. Measures the direct impact of PR efforts on business objectives.

kpis.name: Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)
kpis.description: The total cost directly associated with the production and upkeep of the product or services sold by a company. It provides insights into production efficiency and directly impacts profitability.
kpis.formula:
COGS (retail) = Starting inventory + Purchases + Direct Labor + Direct Factory Overheads - Ending Inventory

COGS (Software/Service Industry) = Direct Labor Costs + Direct Platform/Hosting Costs + Third-party Software License Costs
kpis.example:
A software company pays $10,000 in server costs, $5,000 in cloud fees, $4,000 in customer support, and $2,000 in essential engineering maintenance, resulting in a COGS of $21,000.

In the formula, Direct Labor Costs includes salaries or wages of personnel directly involved in the provision of the service or maintenance of the product/service.

kpis.name: Cost of Incidents
kpis.description: Calculates the total cost associated with incidents, including lost revenue, remediation efforts, and any compensation to customers. This metric quantifies the financial impact of incidents.
kpis.formula: (Total Costs Related to Incidents)
kpis.example: If incident response and lost revenue total $10,000, the cost of incidents is $10,000.

kpis.name: Cost of Office Space per Employee
kpis.description: Calculates the average cost of office space allocated for each employee. With the rise of remote work and flexible working arrangements, understanding this cost can help companies make decisions about real estate, office design, and work policies.
kpis.formula: Total Cost of Office Space / Total Number of In-office Employees
kpis.example: If office space costs $200,000 annually and houses 100 employees, the Cost of Office Space per Employee would be $2,000.

kpis.name: Cost of Transcripts
kpis.description: The expense associated with transcribing each podcast episode. Transcripts can increase the accessibility and SEO value of a podcast, but they come at a cost. Balancing the benefit against the cost is crucial.

kpis.name: Cost Per Acquisition at Each Funnel Stage
kpis.description: The cost incurred to acquire a customer at a specific stage of the funnel. Helps in understanding the financial efficiency of different stages.
kpis.formula: Total cost for that stage / Number of acquisitions at that stage
kpis.example: If $500 is spent on a campaign that results in 50 sign-ups, CPA is $10 per sign-up.

kpis.name: Cost Per Attendee
kpis.description: The total cost of the event divided by the number of attendees. Helps in determining the financial efficiency of the event.
kpis.formula: Total Event Cost / Total Attendees
kpis.example: If an event costs $10,000 and 500 people attend, the cost per attendee is $20.

kpis.name: Cost per Bid
kpis.description: Calculates the average cost incurred in the preparation and submission of a bid, important for budgeting and assessing the cost-efficiency of the bid process.
kpis.formula: (Total Cost of Bid Preparation / Number of Bids)
kpis.example: If the total cost for preparing 10 bids is $5,000, cost per bid is $5,000/10 = $500.

kpis.name: Cost Per Click
kpis.description: Cost Per Click (CPC) represents the average amount of money spent for each click on an ad. It helps manage advertising budget and measure the cost-efficiency of campaigns.
kpis.formula: Total ad spend / Total clicks
kpis.example: If $200 is spent on an ad that receives 100 clicks, the CPC is $2.

kpis.name: Cost Per Conversion
kpis.description: The average cost of each successful conversion from an ad. Measures the cost-effectiveness and ROI of digital advertising campaigns.
kpis.formula: Total ad spend / Number of conversions
kpis.example: If $200 is spent on an ad leading to 20 sign-ups, the Cost Per Conversion is $10.

kpis.name: Cost Per Install
kpis.description: Cost Per Install calculates the average cost incurred to acquire a new user who installs the app, often through advertising. It's a key metric for assessing the efficiency and ROI of app marketing campaigns. A lower cost is generally desirable, indicating cost-effective user acquisition.
kpis.formula: Total Ad Spend / Number of Installs
kpis.example: If $5,000 spent on ads results in 1,000 app installs, the CPI is $5,000 / 1,000 = $5 per install.

kpis.name: Cost Per Thousand (CPM)
kpis.description: The cost of reaching 1,000 audience members with a particular advertisement. Helps in evaluating and comparing the cost-effectiveness of different advertising mediums.
kpis.formula: Total Ad Spend / (Total Impressions / 1000)
kpis.example: If an ad campaign costs $10,000 and reaches 2 million people, the CPM is $5.

kpis.name: Cost Performance Index
kpis.description: Cost Performance Index (CPI) measures the cost efficiency of a project. A CPI below 1 suggests the project is over budget, whereas a CPI above 1 implies it's under budget. CPI aids in forecasting final project costs.
kpis.formula: CPI = Earned Value (EV) / Actual Cost (AC)
kpis.example: If the earned value is $50,000 and the actual cost incurred is $55,000, the CPI is 0.91.

kpis.name: Cost Variance
kpis.description: Cost Variance represents the difference between the expected costs (budgeted) and the actual costs at a given point in the project. This KPI is instrumental in tracking the financial health of the project.
kpis.formula: Cost Variance = Earned Value (EV) - Actual Cost (AC)
kpis.example: If the earned value of a task is $10,000 and the actual cost is $9,000, the cost variance is $1,000.

kpis.name: Cost-Benefit Ratio Estimate
kpis.description: Assesses the financial value of a project relative to its cost, aiding stakeholders in determining the project's economic viability. A higher ratio signifies more benefits relative to costs.
kpis.formula: Cost-Benefit Ratio = Total Benefits (in monetary terms) / Total Costs
kpis.example: If a project yields benefits worth $150,000 and has costs of $100,000, the cost-benefit ratio is 1.5.

kpis.name: CPU Utilization
kpis.description: The percentage of the CPU's capacity that the application uses during execution, impacting the application's performance and server load.
kpis.formula: (CPU Time Used by the Application / Total CPU Time Available) * 100
kpis.example: If an application uses 250 CPU seconds during a period when 1,000 CPU seconds were available, the CPU utilization is (250/1000)*100 = 25%.

kpis.name: CPU Utilization
kpis.description: Measures the percentage of the CPU's capacity utilized by the application during execution, impacting performance and server load.
kpis.formula: (CPU Time Used by the Application / Total CPU Time Available) * 100
kpis.example: If an application uses 250 CPU seconds during a period when 1,000 CPU seconds were available, the CPU utilization is (250/1000)*100 = 25%.

kpis.name: Crisis Response Time
kpis.description: The duration it takes for a company to respond publicly to a negative event or crisis situation, in hours or minutes. A quick response can mitigate potential damage to the brand's reputation.

kpis.name: Current Ratio
kpis.description: Measures a company's ability to pay off its short-term liabilities with short-term assets. A current ratio below 1 suggests that the company might have problems meeting its short-term obligations if they came due at once.
kpis.formula: Current Assets / Current Liabilities
kpis.example: If a company has current assets of $1,000,000 and current liabilities of $500,000, its current ratio is 2:1.

kpis.name: Customer Acceptance Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of deliverables that are accepted by the customer without revisions or rework. A high rate suggests that deliverables are meeting customer expectations.
kpis.formula: (Number of accepted deliverables / Total deliverables) x 100
kpis.example: If 90 out of 100 deliverables are accepted by the customer as-is, the acceptance rate is 90%.

kpis.name: Customer Acquisition Cost
