kpis.description: The cost associated with acquiring a new customer. Indicates the efficiency and effectiveness of marketing efforts. Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) typically includes all marketing and sales costs.
kpis.formula: Total Cost of Acquisition / Number of Customers Acquired
kpis.example: If $10,000 is spent on marketing and sales (including advertising, content, events, tools, training, etc) and it results in 200 new customers, the CAC is $50.

kpis.name: Customer Acquisition Cost Payback Period
kpis.description: Time it takes to earn back the Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC) through customer spending. Shows how long it takes for an investment in acquiring a customer to become profitable.
kpis.formula: CAC / Monthly Gross Margin per Customer
kpis.example: If the CAC is $300 and monthly gross margin per customer is $100, the payback period is 3 months.

kpis.name: Customer Callbacks
kpis.description: The number of times customers request a callback after an initial service interaction. Frequent callbacks can indicate unresolved issues or ineffective initial service, which can strain resources and diminish customer satisfaction.
kpis.formula: Total number of customer callback requests
kpis.example: If there were 15 callback requests in a day, the Customer Callbacks count is 15.

kpis.name: Customer Churn Rate
kpis.description:
Measures the percentage of customers or subscribers who stop using a company's product or service during a particular time period. High churn can indicate customer dissatisfaction, while low churn suggests that customers are happy with the product or service.

Businesses should always aim to reduce churn, increase lifetime value, and keep an eye on how much it costs to replace lost customers (Customer Acquisition Cost). If the cost to replace customers becomes higher than the value derived from them, the churn rate is becoming unsustainable.
kpis.formula: (Number of customers lost in period / Total customers at start of period) x 100
kpis.example: If a business started the month with 120 customers and lost 5, the churn rate = (5 / 120) x 100 = 4.17%.

kpis.name: Customer Expansion Rate
kpis.description: Measures the rate at which existing customers expand their contracts or purchases. In businesses where upselling or cross-selling is possible, a high expansion rate indicates that customers see value in the offerings, leading to increased revenue without acquiring new customers.
kpis.formula: (Revenue from upselling / Total revenue) x 100
kpis.example: If you made $10,000 from upselling and the total revenue was $100,000, the expansion rate = (10,000 / 100,000) x 100 = 10%.

kpis.name: Customer Impact
kpis.description: Evaluates how incidents affect customers, considering factors like downtime, data loss, or reduced functionality. A higher score indicates a more significant impact on customers.
kpis.formula: Assessment Score Based on Impact Factors
kpis.example: If an incident causes 2 hours of downtime for many customers, it might be given a high impact score.

kpis.name: Customer Journey Length
kpis.description: The total number of stages or steps in the customer's journey. Helps in understanding the complexity and length of the buying process.
kpis.formula: Count of individual stages or steps in the journey.
kpis.example: A journey with stages like Awareness, Consideration, Purchase, and Loyalty would have a length of 4.

kpis.name: Customer Lifetime Value
kpis.description: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) is a prediction of the total value of a customer to your business over the entirety of their relationship with you. For SaaS companies, this is crucial as it aids in making significant decisions regarding sales, marketing, product development, and customer support.
kpis.formula: (Average Revenue Per Customer (ARPC) * Gross Profit Margin) / Customer Churn Rate
kpis.example: If a SaaS company's ARPC is $100, the gross profit margin is 80% (or 0.8), and the customer churn rate is 5% (or 0.05), then the CLV is $1,600. This means the business can expect to generate $1,600 from an account over its lifespan, on average.

kpis.name: Customer Profitability Score
kpis.description: Identifies and quantifies the profitability of individual customers. Allows companies to tailor their strategies for different customer segments based on profitability.
kpis.formula: Typically a score derived from factors like revenue generated, costs associated, length of relationship, etc.
kpis.example: If a company uses a scoring system from 1-10, a customer generating high revenue with low support costs might score a 9.

kpis.name: Customer Retention Cost
kpis.description: Represents the total cost of activities and efforts aimed at retaining existing customers. A high CRC might indicate that a company is spending a lot to keep customers, which could be due to product issues or stiff competition. Comparing CRC to customer lifetime value can provide insights into the return on retention efforts.
kpis.formula: Total Retention Spending / Number of Customers Retained
kpis.example:
If $10,000 was spent on retention and 500 customers were retained, CRC = $10,000 / 500 = $20 per customer.

Total Retention Spending may include Customer Success and Service costs, training and onboarding costs, loyalty and rewards programs, nurture campaigns, feedback and survey Costs, renewal processes, product upgrade assistance, and community building.

kpis.name: Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)
kpis.description: Measures the average satisfaction of customers with a particular experience, usually on a scale from 1 (very unsatisfied) to 5 (very satisfied). CSAT directly reflects customer contentment and is a primary gauge of service quality. It helps pinpoint areas of improvement.
kpis.formula: (Number of Satisfied Customers (rating 4 and 5) / Number of Survey Responses) * 100
kpis.example: If out of 100 responses, you received a total score of 420 on a scale of 5, your CSAT would be 84%.

kpis.name: Customer Support Agent Feedback
kpis.description: The average feedback score or rating received specifically for the support agent’s performance. This metric assesses agent performance from the customer's perspective, helping to improve training and recognizing outstanding performers.
kpis.formula: Total agent feedback score / Number of feedbacks received
kpis.example: If an agent receives scores of 4, 5, 4, and 4 out of 5 from four customers, their average feedback is 4.25.

kpis.name: Customer Touchpoints Before Conversion
kpis.description: The average number of interactions a customer has with the brand before conversion. Provides insights into the complexity of the buying journey.
kpis.formula: Total number of interactions across all converted customers / Number of converted customers
kpis.example: If 500 interactions lead to 50 conversions, the average is 10 touchpoints per conversion.

kpis.name: Customers With Service Level Agreements in Place
kpis.description: The number of customers who have a formal service level agreement (SLA) set with the company. SLAs often represent higher-tier or more committed customers. Tracking this number can provide insights into a business’s contractual obligations and potential revenue streams.
kpis.formula: Total number of customers with active SLAs
kpis.example: If a company has 150 customers, and 50 of them have SLAs, then the number of customers with SLAs is 50.

kpis.name: Cycle Count Accuracy
kpis.description: Assesses the accuracy of inventory counts done periodically (daily, weekly, monthly) compared to the inventory management system records. Regular cycle counts help in maintaining inventory accuracy, identifying potential issues early, and reducing the need for full, disruptive inventory checks.
kpis.formula: (Items Correctly Counted in Cycle Count / Total Items in Cycle Count) x 100
kpis.example: If 485 out of 500 items are correctly counted in a cycle count, the accuracy is 97%.

kpis.name: Cycle Time
kpis.description: Cycle Time calculates the time taken to complete a task once it has started, in hours or days. This KPI provides insights into process efficiency and aids in identifying areas of improvement.
kpis.formula: Cycle Time = Date of task completion - Date of task start
kpis.example: If a task commenced on January 5th and was finished on January 8th, the cycle time is 3 days.

kpis.name: Daily Active Users (DAU)
kpis.description: DAU counts the number of unique users who engage with the software on a daily basis. It's a direct measure of the software's ability to attract and retain users daily. High DAU indicates strong user engagement and a habitual use of the product. This metric is essential for understanding daily user engagement patterns and for measuring the impact of new features or updates.
kpis.formula: Count of Unique Users Per Day
kpis.example: If 5,000 users interact with a software each day, the DAU is 5,000.

kpis.name: Damaged Stock Rate
kpis.description: Represents the percentage of stock that gets damaged. It provides insights into the quality of storage and handling.
kpis.formula: (Number of Damaged Stock Items / Total Stock Items) x 100
kpis.example: If 50 out of 2000 items are damaged, the rate is 2.5%.

kpis.name: Data Breaches Involving Sensitive Data
kpis.description: Counts the number of data breaches where sensitive or confidential data was exposed. Breaches involving sensitive data can have significant legal, financial, and reputational implications.
kpis.formula: Count of data breaches involving sensitive data
kpis.example: If there were 3 breaches involving sensitive data in a year, the KPI would be 3.

kpis.name: Data Completeness
kpis.description: Data Completeness evaluates the extent to which necessary data is available for model training. It’s vital for ensuring the model is trained on comprehensive and representative data.
kpis.formula: (Number of Complete Records / Total Records) * 100
kpis.example: If 950 out of 1,000 records are complete, the data completeness is (950/1000)*100 = 95%.

kpis.name: Data Diversity Index
kpis.description: Measures the diversity in the training dataset, ensuring that the model is exposed to a wide range of scenarios. High diversity can improve the model’s applicability and reduce bias.
kpis.formula: Diversity Assessment Methodology
kpis.example: For example, a diversity score may be calculated based on the variety of data sources, types, and demographic representation.

kpis.name: Data Pipeline Processing Time
kpis.description: This KPI tracks the time taken for data to move through the entire pipeline, from collection and processing to being ready for use in model training. Efficiency here is key for timely model updates and iterations.
kpis.formula: Total Time for Data Pipeline Processing
kpis.example: If data takes 5 hours to be processed and ready for training, the processing time is 5 hours.

kpis.name: Data Throughput
kpis.description: Measures the amount of data processed per unit of time in the data pipeline, indicating the pipeline's efficiency and capacity.
kpis.formula: (Amount of Data Processed / Processing Time)
kpis.example: If 100GB of data is processed in 10 hours, the throughput is 10GB per hour.

kpis.name: Days From Employment Request to Job Opening
kpis.description:
Tracks the time taken from the identification of a staffing need to the official announcement of a job vacancy. It's a measure of internal HR responsiveness.

Delays in announcing vacancies can impact business operations, especially if a department is waiting to execute projects pending new hires. It's a reflection of the HR department's efficiency and alignment with business needs.
kpis.formula: (Date job is opened - Date employment request was made)
kpis.example: If HR takes 5 days from request to job posting, this reflects a fairly rapid response.

kpis.name: Days Payable Outstanding
kpis.description: Represents the average number of days it takes a company to pay its invoices from trade creditors, such as suppliers. Days Payable Outstanding (DPO) can offer insights into the company’s liquidity and how efficiently it manages its obligations. Longer DPO might indicate that a company is trying to conserve its cash.
kpis.formula: (Accounts Payable / Cost of Goods Sold) x Number of Days
kpis.example: If accounts payable is $5,000 and COGS is $25,000 for a 30-day month, DPO = (5,000 / 25,000) x 30 = 6 days.

kpis.name: Days Sales Outstanding
kpis.description: Measures the average number of days it takes a company to collect payment after a sale has been made. Days Sales Outstanding (DSO) can indicate the efficiency of the company's accounts receivable and the quality of its customer base. High DSO may mean longer cash conversion cycles or potential issues with credit policies.
kpis.formula: (Accounts Receivable / Total Credit Sales) x Number of Days
kpis.example: If accounts receivable is $10,000 and total credit sales are $20,000 for a 30-day month, DSO = (10,000 / 20,000) x 30 = 15 days.

kpis.name: Deal Slippage Rate
kpis.description: Indicates the rate at which deals are postponed or pushed out beyond their expected close date, impacting sales forecasting.
kpis.formula: (Number of Deals Slipped / Total Number of Deals) * 100
kpis.example: If 10 out of 100 deals slip, the slippage rate is (10/100)*100 = 10%.

kpis.name: Deal Win/Loss Ratio
kpis.description: Compares the number of won deals to lost deals, providing insight into sales effectiveness.
kpis.formula: Number of Won Deals / Number of Lost Deals
kpis.example: If a team wins 30 deals and loses 20, the win/loss ratio is 30/20 = 1.5.

kpis.name: Debt Service Coverage Ratio
kpis.description: Measures a company's ability to service its current debts with its current cash flow. A higher ratio means the company is more capable of meeting its debt obligations, reducing the risk for creditors.
kpis.formula: Operating Income / Total Debt Service
kpis.example: If a company has an operating income of $500,000 and debt obligations of $250,000, the Debt Service Coverage Ratio would be 2:1.

kpis.name: Debt-to-Equity Ratio
kpis.description: Compares a company's total debt to its total equity, giving insights into its financial leverage. A high ratio might indicate that the company is heavily financed by debt, which can be riskier.
kpis.formula: Total Debt / Total Equity
kpis.example: If a company has a total debt of $1,000,000 and total equity of $2,000,000, its debt-to-equity ratio is 0.5:1.

kpis.name: Defect Escape Rate
kpis.description: Measures the percentage of defects that escape into production, signifying the effectiveness of pre-release testing.
kpis.formula: (Number of Defects Found in Production / Total Number of Defects) * 100
kpis.example: If 10 out of 60 defects are found post-release, the escape rate is (10/60)*100 = 16.67%.

kpis.name: Deferred Revenue
kpis.description: Revenue received by a company for goods or services that haven't been provided yet. It indicates future obligations and gives insights into the company's cash flow and future revenue recognition.
kpis.formula: Payments received in advance - recognized revenue
kpis.example: If a software company collects a yearly subscription fee of $1200 upfront but has delivered only one month of service, the deferred revenue is $1100

kpis.name: Demand Forecasting
kpis.description: Predicts the quantity of products that customers will purchase, aiding in managing production and inventory levels.
kpis.formula: (Sum of Historical Demand Data + Trend Analysis) / Periods
kpis.example: With a historical demand of 10,000 units over 10 periods and a 5% upward trend, the forecast for the next period is (10,000/10) * 1.05 = 1,050 units.

kpis.name: Demo or Presentation Rate
kpis.description: Measures the percentage of sales presentations or demos given out of the total number of opportunities, showing engagement level and value proposition delivery.
kpis.formula: (Number of Demos or Presentations / Number of Opportunities) * 100
kpis.example: For 80 demos given in 200 opportunities, the rate is (80/200)*100 = 40%.

kpis.name: Deployment Frequency
kpis.description: Measures the rate of software deployments over a specified period. High deployment frequency is typically a sign of an efficient and agile development process, enabling quicker feature releases and faster response to market changes.
kpis.formula: (Number of Deployments / Time Period)
kpis.example: For example, if a team deploys updates 30 times over a month, the deployment frequency is 30.

kpis.name: Device Usage
kpis.description: Device Usage identifies the types and models of devices used by the app's audience. Understanding which devices are most popular among users helps in optimizing the app's performance and design for those specific devices.
kpis.formula: List of Devices Used by Users
kpis.example: If the majority of users are on mid-range Android devices, the app should be optimized for performance on these devices.

kpis.name: Devices and Operating Systems
kpis.description: Tracks the variety of devices and operating systems used by the app’s user base. This metric helps in optimizing the app’s performance and design for the most commonly used devices and OSes.
kpis.formula: List of Devices and Operating Systems
kpis.example: Common devices might include various models of smartphones and tablets, while OSes might include Android, iOS, Windows.

kpis.name: Dividend Payout Ratio
kpis.description: Assesses the proportion of net income paid out to shareholders in the form of dividends. It provides insight into how much profit is being returned to shareholders versus being reinvested in the company. A high ratio might indicate generous returns to shareholders, but it might also mean less reinvestment in the business.
kpis.formula: (Dividends Paid / Net Income) x 100
kpis.example: If a company has net income of $500,000 and pays out $100,000 in dividends, the Dividend Payout Ratio would be 20%.

kpis.name: Documentation Coverage
kpis.description: Measures the extent to which the codebase is documented. Adequate documentation is essential for maintainability and ease of understanding.
kpis.formula: (Lines of Documentation / Total Lines of Code) * 100
kpis.example: If there are 500 lines of documentation for 2,000 lines of code, the documentation coverage is (500/2000)*100 = 25%.

kpis.name: Documentation Coverage
kpis.description: Assesses the extent to which the codebase is documented. Adequate documentation supports maintainability and facilitates onboarding new team members.
kpis.formula: (Lines of Documentation / Total Lines of Code) * 100
kpis.example: If there are 500 lines of documentation for 2,000 lines of code, the documentation coverage is (500/2000)*100 = 25%.

kpis.name: Downloads per Episode
kpis.description: The average number of downloads each episode receives. Allows you to gauge which episodes are the most popular and resonates most with your audience.

kpis.name: Drop-Off Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of individuals who leave the funnel at a particular stage. Understanding where customers drop off can highlight areas of improvement.
kpis.formula: (Number of individuals who left at a stage / Total number at that stage) x 100
kpis.example: If 100 visitors start a sign-up form but only 70 complete it, the drop-off rate is 30%.

kpis.name: Earnings Before Interest and Taxes (EBIT)
kpis.description: A company's net income before interest and income tax expenses are considered. EBIT provides a clear picture of a company's operational profitability, excluding the impact of finance and tax strategies.
kpis.formula: Net Income + Interest + Taxes
kpis.example: If a company's net income is $500,000, with interest expenses of $50,000 and tax expenses of $100,000, the EBIT is $650,000.

kpis.name: Earnings Per Share
kpis.description: Represents the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share of common stock. It's a widely used metric for evaluating a company's profitability relative to its shareholders. Rising EPS can attract investors, while a declining EPS may raise concerns.
kpis.formula: (Net Income - Dividends on Preferred Stock) / Average Outstanding Shares
kpis.example: If net income is $50,000, no dividends on preferred stock, and there are 10,000 shares, EPS = $50,000 / 10,000 = $5/share.

kpis.name: Economic Order Quantity
kpis.description: EOQ provides the ideal order quantity to minimize costs related to order, holding, and shortage costs. It's foundational for efficient inventory management.
kpis.formula: Square Root of [(2 x Demand x Order Cost) / Holding Cost]
kpis.example: For an annual demand of 1200 units, order cost of $5, and holding cost of $2 per unit, the EOQ is about 49 units.

kpis.name: Effective Tax Rate
kpis.description: Represents the average rate at which a corporation or individual is taxed on their pre-tax income. Effective Tax Rate offers a clear picture of the actual tax rate being paid by the company. This may differ from the nominal tax rate due to deductions, credits, and other factors.
kpis.formula: (Total Tax Expense / Taxable Income) x 100
kpis.example: If a company has a taxable income of $500,000 and a total tax expense of $150,000, its effective tax rate would be 30%.

kpis.name: Email Backlog
kpis.description: The number of unresolved or unopened emails at any given time. A growing backlog might indicate understaffing, inefficient processes, or an influx of complicated issues. It's crucial to prevent prolonged response times which can harm customer satisfaction.
kpis.formula: Total number of unresolved emails
kpis.example: If 25 emails remain unaddressed at the end of the day, the Email Backlog is 25.

kpis.name: Email Case Reopen Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of email cases that were marked as resolved but had to be reopened due to the issue not being adequately addressed. A high reopen rate can indicate that issues are not being effectively solved on the first attempt, possibly leading to customer frustration and diminished trust.
kpis.formula: (Number of reopened email cases / Total resolved email cases) x 100
kpis.example: If 5 out of 100 resolved cases had to be reopened, the Email Case Reopen Rate is 5%.

kpis.name: Email Click-Through Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of recipients who click on a link within the email. Indicates the relevance and appeal of the content or call-to-action in your email.
kpis.formula: (Number of Clicks / Number of Emails Opened) x 100
kpis.example: If 200 people open an email and 40 click a link, the CTR is 20%.

kpis.name: Email Conversion Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of email recipients who take a desired action, such as making a purchase or signing up for a webinar. Measures the effectiveness of your email in driving desired outcomes.
kpis.formula: (Number of Conversions / Number of Emails Sent) x 100
kpis.example: If 1,000 emails are sent and 30 recipients make a purchase, the conversion rate is 3%.

kpis.name: Email Delivery Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of emails that reach the recipient's inbox without being filtered out. Ensures that your emails aren't being wrongly categorized as spam or junk.
kpis.formula: (Number of Emails Delivered / Number of Emails Sent) x 100
kpis.example: If 950 emails out of 1,000 reach the inbox, the delivery rate is 95%.

kpis.name: Email Open Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of recipients who open the email newsletter. Reflects the effectiveness of your email subject line and sender reputation.
kpis.formula: (Number of Emails Opened / Number of Emails Sent) x 100
kpis.example: If you send 1,000 emails and 200 are opened, the open rate is 20%.

kpis.name: Email Return on Investment
kpis.description: Measures the profitability of your newsletter or email marketing campaigns. Helps determine if the resources invested are generating a profitable return.
kpis.formula: [(Revenue from Email - Cost of Email Campaign) / Cost of Email Campaign] x 100
kpis.example: If an email campaign costs $500 and brings in $2,500, the ROI is 400%.

kpis.name: Email Template Usage Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of emails sent using predefined templates versus manually written emails. A high template usage rate can indicate efficient responses for common queries. However, over-reliance on templates might suggest a lack of personalization or adaptability to unique customer issues.
kpis.formula: (Number of templated emails sent / Total emails sent) x 100
kpis.example: If 40 out of 50 emails sent were templated, the Email Template Usage Rate is 80%.

kpis.name: Email Volume
kpis.description: Email Volume measures the number of emails or inquiries your team receives via email during a specific time period. It can help understand the demand for support and gauge the adequacy of your support resources.
kpis.formula: Count of customer support tickets received during a specified period
kpis.example: If 150 customer emails were received on a particular day, the Email Volume for that day is 150.

kpis.name: Emails Per Resolution
kpis.description: The average number of email interactions required to resolve a customer's issue. Fewer emails per resolution imply efficient problem-solving and clearer communication, leading to enhanced customer satisfaction.
kpis.formula: Total emails exchanged / Number of resolved cases
kpis.example: If 60 emails were exchanged to resolve 20 cases, the Emails Per Resolution is 3.

kpis.name: Employee Feedback Action Rate
kpis.description:
The frequency with which employee feedback, given through various channels, results in tangible change within the organization.

It measures the organization's responsiveness to employee input. Implementing feedback enhances trust and demonstrates to employees that their opinions are valued and acted upon.
kpis.formula: (Number of feedback items acted upon / Total feedback items received) x 100
kpis.example: If the company acted on 50 out of 200 feedback items, the rate is 25%.

kpis.name: Employee Feedback Score on Training Effectiveness
kpis.description: Reflects the average score given by employees on the perceived effectiveness of the training sessions they've attended. Direct feedback can help identify areas for improvement in training content or methods.
kpis.formula: Total feedback scores received / Number of feedback forms submitted
kpis.example: If the total score from 100 feedback forms is 800, the average feedback score is 8.

kpis.name: Employee Issue Resolution Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of reported employee issues or concerns that get resolved within a specified timeframe. Resolving employee issues promptly and effectively boosts morale, reduces friction, and creates a more harmonious work environment. A high resolution rate reflects a responsive and attentive HR or management team.
kpis.formula: (Number of resolved issues / Total number of reported issues) x 100
kpis.example: If there are 200 reported issues and 180 get resolved, the resolution rate is 90%.

kpis.name: Employee Retention
kpis.description:
The percentage of employees who remain with the company over a specified period, typically a year. It provides insights into the organization's ability to maintain its workforce.

High retention rates typically indicate that employees are satisfied and the company offers a conducive environment for growth and productivity. Low retention can signal underlying issues and result in increased costs related to hiring and training replacements.
kpis.formula: (Number of employees at end of period - Number of employees hired during the period) / Number of employees at start of period x 100
kpis.example:
If a company starts with 100 employees, hires 20 new ones, and ends with 105 employees, the retention rate is 85%.

Typically, startups can have a retention rate of anywhere between 60% to 85%. Larger companies often see retention rates between 85% to 95% or even higher.

kpis.name: Employee Satisfaction in On-call Duties
kpis.description: Gauges the satisfaction level of employees with on-call responsibilities, reflecting the workload, stress level, and overall work-life balance.
kpis.formula: Survey or Feedback Score
kpis.example: If on-call staff rate their satisfaction as 8 out of 10 on average, the satisfaction score is 8.

kpis.name: Employee Satisfaction Score
kpis.description:
Represents the overall satisfaction levels of employees typically derived from periodic satisfaction surveys.

Job satisfaction is intrinsically linked to productivity, retention, and overall workplace morale. Identifying and addressing areas of dissatisfaction can help prevent turnover and improve performance.

An Employee Satisfaction Survey typically covers a broad range of topics to assess how employees feel about various aspects of their work environment, job responsibilities, and the company culture.
kpis.formula: Varies based on survey method. Could be average score or percentage satisfied.
kpis.example: An average score of 8.5 out of 10 across all employees.

kpis.name: Employee Tenure Average
kpis.description:
The average duration of service among current employees, usually measured in years. It offers insights into the company's stability and workforce experience.

Long tenure averages suggest employee satisfaction, while shorter tenures might indicate issues in retention or a younger, more transient workforce. This KPI provides insights into the depth of institutional knowledge within the organization.
kpis.formula: Total years of service for all current employees / Number of current employees
kpis.example: If a company has 100 employees with a combined total of 500 years of service, the average tenure is 5 years.

kpis.name: Employees Trained in First Aid
kpis.description: The percentage of employees who have received first aid training. A higher percentage indicates a workplace better prepared to deal with emergencies, ensuring quicker response times during accidents, and potentially saving lives.
kpis.formula: (Number of Employees Trained in First Aid / Total Number of Employees) x 100
kpis.example: If 50 out of 250 employees are trained in first aid, the rate would be 20%.

kpis.name: Employees With Disability
kpis.description: The number or percentage of employees who identify as having a disability. Companies committed to inclusivity should ensure that they are accessible and accommodating to employees with disabilities. This metric highlights the company's dedication to a diverse and inclusive work environment.
kpis.formula: (Number of Employees with Disabilities / Total Number of Employees) x 100 for percentage.
kpis.example: In a company of 500 employees, if 25 employees have a disability, the rate is 5%.

kpis.name: Engagement on PR Content
kpis.description: The number of likes, shares, comments, or interactions PR content receives on social media or other platforms. Indicates the resonance and impact of the PR content with the audience.

kpis.name: Engagement Rate
kpis.description: Measures the level of engagement that a piece of content is receiving from the audience. Indicates how relevant or interesting content is to your audience. Higher engagement typically correlates with broader reach and more impactful messaging. You may break this metric down by engagement type and separately track likes rate, comments rate, and shares / reposts rate.
kpis.formula: (Total Engagements / Total Followers) x 100
kpis.example: If a post gets 200 likes and comments, and you have 10,000 followers, the engagement rate is 2%.

kpis.name: Equity Turnover
kpis.description: Evaluates a company's efficiency in using shareholders' equity to generate sales. A high turnover indicates that a company is using its equity more efficiently to produce sales.
kpis.formula: Net Sales / Average Shareholders' Equity
kpis.example: With net sales of $2 million and average shareholder equity of $500,000, the Equity Turnover would be 4 times.

kpis.name: Error Budget Burn Rate
kpis.description: Measures the rate at which the error budget (the acceptable threshold of unreliability) is consumed. Monitoring this helps ensure that the service does not exceed the permissible level of unreliability.
kpis.formula: (Consumed Error Budget / Total Error Budget)
kpis.example: If you have an error budget of 1% downtime and currently are at 0.5% downtime, the burn rate is 0.5 / 1 = 50%.

kpis.name: Error Rate
kpis.description: Represents the percentage of project deliverables with defects or errors. A lower error rate is indicative of higher quality deliverables.
kpis.formula: (Number of deliverables with defects / Total deliverables) x 100
kpis.example: If 2 out of 10 deliverables have defects, the error rate is 20%.

kpis.name: Escalation Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of customer service requests that are escalated to higher-level support tiers or management. A high escalation rate can indicate frontline support inefficiencies, training gaps, product deficiencies, or complex issues that need specialized attention.
kpis.formula: (Number of escalated cases / Total number of cases) x 100
kpis.example: If 10 out of 100 customer cases are escalated, the escalation rate is 10%.

kpis.name: Escalation Rate
kpis.description: The frequency at which incidents are escalated to higher-level teams or management, indicating the complexity of incidents and potential gaps in initial response capabilities.
kpis.formula: (Number of Escalated Incidents / Total Number of Incidents) * 100
kpis.example: If 5 out of 50 incidents are escalated, the escalation rate is (5/50)*100 = 10%.

kpis.name: Estimate At Completion
kpis.description: Estimate At Completion (EAC) is a forecast of the most likely total project cost based on the project's performance and risk quantification. It provides stakeholders with an idea of the expected final cost of the project.
kpis.formula: EAC = Actual Cost (AC) + Bottom-Up Estimate to Complete (ETC)
kpis.example: If a project has spent $50,000 so far and the remaining work is estimated to cost $200,000, the EAC is $250,000.

kpis.name: Estimate To Complete
kpis.description: Estimate To Complete (ETC) provides an estimate of the remaining costs to complete the project. This metric helps project managers adjust budgets and allocate resources efficiently.
kpis.formula: ETC = Estimate at Completion (EAC) - Actual Cost (AC)
kpis.example: If the EAC for a project is $250,000 and $50,000 has already been spent, the ETC is $200,000.

kpis.name: Event Feedback Score
kpis.description: An average score from post-event surveys or feedback forms. Provides qualitative insights into what went well and what needs improvement.

kpis.name: Event Return on Investment
kpis.description: Measures the profitability of the event against its cost. Demonstrates the financial success of the event.
kpis.formula: [(Revenue from Event - Cost of Event) / Cost of Event] x 100
kpis.example: If the event costs $10,000 and brings in $30,000, the ROI is 200%.

kpis.name: External to Internal Contribution Ratio
kpis.description: Evaluates the balance between contributions from external community members versus internal team members. A higher ratio suggests strong community involvement and external interest, which is vital for open source project sustainability.
kpis.formula: (Number of External Contributions / Number of Internal Contributions)
kpis.example: If a project receives 200 external contributions and 100 internal contributions, the ratio is 200/100 = 2.

kpis.name: Feature Importance Score
kpis.description: Feature Importance Score evaluates the impact of different input features on the model’s predictions. It’s used to identify which features are most influential in the model's decision-making process.
kpis.formula: Feature Importance Analysis
kpis.example: In a loan approval model, credit score might have a feature importance score of 0.7, indicating a high impact on the model’s decisions.

kpis.name: Fill Rate
kpis.description: Represents the percentage of customer orders that are fulfilled from stock on hand. A lower rate may lead to backorders and customer dissatisfaction.
kpis.formula: (Number of Orders Fulfilled from Stock / Total Customer Orders) x 100
kpis.example: If 950 out of 1000 customer orders are fulfilled from stock, the fill rate is 95%.

kpis.name: First Contact Resolution Rate
kpis.description: Percentage of customer service interactions that resolved the customer's issue or concern in the initial contact. Resolving issues on first contact can enhance customer satisfaction and reduce operational costs.
kpis.formula: (Number of issues resolved on the first contact / Total number of resolved issues) * 100
kpis.example: If 75 out of 100 issues were resolved on the first contact, the rate is 75%.

kpis.name: First Response Time
kpis.description: First Response Time is the average amount of time it takes for a customer to receive the first human response to their inquiry. It's a crucial metric for assessing the efficiency and speed of a customer support team. A shorter first response time can lead to improved customer satisfaction.
kpis.formula: Total time taken for first responses / Number of customer inquiries
kpis.example: If in one day, the first responses to 10 inquiries took a combined total of 30 minutes, the average First Response Time is 3 minutes.

kpis.name: Flaky Tests
kpis.description: Flaky tests are those that produce inconsistent results each time they are run. They can undermine confidence in the testing process and lead to overlooked defects.
kpis.formula: Count of Tests with Inconsistent Results
kpis.example: If 5 tests out of a suite of 100 exhibit flaky behavior, the count is 5.

kpis.name: Follow-Up Contact Rate
kpis.description: Tracks the rate at which sales reps follow up with leads after the initial contact, indicating thoroughness of the sales process.
kpis.formula: (Number of Follow-Up Contacts / Number of Initial Contacts) * 100
kpis.example: If there are 150 follow-ups for 200 initial contacts, the follow-up rate is (150/200)*100 = 75%.

kpis.name: Follower Growth Rate
kpis.description: Calculates the rate at which your social media profiles are gaining followers. Indicates brand popularity, awareness, and the effectiveness of social media strategies.
kpis.formula: ((Number of followers at end of period - Number of followers at start of period) / Number of followers at start of period) x 100
kpis.example: If you started the month with 5,000 followers and ended with 5,500, your growth rate would be 10%.

kpis.name: Forwarding or Social Sharing Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of recipients who forward the email or share its content on social media. Indicates the value and shareability of your content.
kpis.formula: (Number of Shares or Forwards / Number of Emails Sent) x 100
kpis.example: If 1,000 emails are sent and 10 are shared or forwarded, the rate is 1%.

kpis.name: Frequency of Blog Posts
kpis.description: How often new content is published on the blog. Regular content publishing can boost SEO, keep readers engaged, and establish the brand as a thought leader.
kpis.formula: Number of posts / Time period
kpis.example: If a blog publishes 8 posts in a month, the frequency is 2 per week.

kpis.name: Frequency of Canceled Orders
kpis.description: Highlights the number of orders canceled, either due to stockouts or other operational inefficiencies. High numbers can indicate forecasting or operational issues.
kpis.formula: Total Number of Orders Canceled in a Period
kpis.example: If 10 orders are canceled in a month due to stockouts, the frequency is 10.

kpis.name: Frequency of Data Backups
kpis.description: Indicates how often data backups are conducted. Regular backups are a key part of disaster recovery and ensuring data integrity.
kpis.formula: Stated as a set interval (e.g., daily, every 12 hours)
kpis.example: If backups are taken every 24 hours, the frequency is daily.

kpis.name: Frequency of Documentation Reviews vs Set Schedule
kpis.description: Measures how often documentation is reviewed compared to the organization's predefined schedule. Regular documentation reviews help identify and rectify any discrepancies or outdated information.
kpis.formula: (Number of actual documentation reviews / Number of scheduled documentation reviews) x 100
kpis.example: If there were 8 actual reviews and 10 scheduled reviews in a year, the frequency is 80%.

kpis.name: Frequency of Emergency Orders
kpis.description: This KPI measures how often emergency orders are placed, indicating potential inefficiencies in order prediction or supplier reliability.
kpis.formula: Total Number of Emergency Orders Placed in a Period
kpis.example: If a business places 5 emergency orders in a month, the frequency is 5.

kpis.name: Frequency of Episodes
kpis.description: The number of podcast episodes published within a specific timeframe. Consistency in episode release can enhance audience engagement and loyalty.

kpis.name: Frequency of Financial Reporting
kpis.description: How often financial reports are generated and distributed (monthly or quarterly). Regular reporting helps with timely decision-making and demonstrates transparency.
kpis.formula: Count of reports generated within a specific time frame.
kpis.example: If reports are generated monthly, the frequency is "12 per year".

kpis.name: Frequency of videos
kpis.description: The number of videos released in a specific timeframe. Provides insights into content production pace and consistency.

kpis.name: Funnel Conversion Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of top-funnel prospects who become customers. Indicates the overall effectiveness of the entire customer journey.
kpis.formula: (Number of conversions at the end of the funnel / Total top-funnel traffic) x 100
kpis.example: If 1,000 visitors lead to 50 purchases, the funnel conversion rate is 5%.

kpis.name: GDPR, CCPA, and other Data Requests Processed Within the Time Frame
kpis.description: Measures the percentage of data requests under regulations like GDPR or CCPA that are processed within the stipulated time frame. Complying with regulatory timelines is critical to avoid penalties and maintain trust.
kpis.formula: (Number of data requests processed on time / Total number of data requests) x 100
kpis.example: If 90 out of 100 GDPR data requests are processed within the deadline, the rate is 90%.

kpis.name: Gender Ratio
kpis.description: Denotes the proportion of male to female employees in the organization. A balanced gender ratio can lead to diverse viewpoints, fostering creativity and innovation. It also ensures that there's no gender disparity in hiring.
kpis.formula: Number of Male Employees : Number of Female Employees
kpis.example: If there are 60 male and 40 female employees, the gender ratio is 3:2.

kpis.name: Gross Margin Return on Inventory Investment
kpis.description: This metric evaluates the return on every dollar invested in inventory. A higher GMROII indicates effective inventory purchasing and sales.
kpis.formula: (Gross Margin / Average Inventory Cost)
kpis.example: If the gross margin is $100,000 and the average inventory cost is $50,000, the GMROII is 2.

kpis.name: Gross Profit Margin
kpis.description: A financial metric that shows a company's financial health by revealing the proportion of money left over from revenues after accounting for the cost of goods sold (COGS). A high gross profit margin indicates that a company can make a reasonable profit on sales, as long as it keeps overhead costs in control.
kpis.formula: (Gross Profit / Total Revenue) * 100
kpis.example: If a company's gross profit is $400,000 and its revenue is $1,000,000, the gross profit margin is 40%. This means the company retains 40% of its total revenue after accounting for the direct costs associated with producing the goods it sells.

kpis.name: Herfindahl-Hirschman Index
kpis.description: A measure of market concentration that sums the squares of the market shares of all firms in the industry, indicating market competitiveness.
kpis.formula: Sum of the Squares of Individual Market Shares
kpis.example: If there are four firms with shares of 30%, 30%, 20%, and 20%, HHI is (0.3² + 0.3² + 0.2² + 0.2²) = 0.26.

kpis.name: High-potential Employee Retention Rate
kpis.description: Indicates the retention rate specifically for employees identified as having high potential for future leadership roles. Retaining high-potential employees is crucial for the future growth and leadership pipeline of the organization.
kpis.formula: (Number of High-potential Employees Retained / Total Number of High-potential Employees) x 100
kpis.example: If out of 50 high-potential employees, 45 are retained, the retention rate would be 90%.

kpis.name: Holding Cost per Unit
kpis.description: Represents the average cost to hold each unit of inventory. It's crucial for pricing decisions and understanding the true cost of inventory.
kpis.formula: Total Inventory Holding Costs / Total Units in Inventory
kpis.example: If total holding costs are $10,000 and there are 5,000 units in inventory, the holding cost per unit is $2.

kpis.name: Hours of 1 on 1 coaching time per agent
kpis.description: Average number of hours each agent spends in one-on-one coaching or training sessions over a specific period. Regular coaching can improve agent skills, boost morale, and ultimately lead to better customer service outcomes.
kpis.formula: Total coaching hours / Number of agents
kpis.example: If 5 agents receive a combined total of 25 hours of coaching in a month, the average is 5 hours per agent.

kpis.name: HR Budget Utilization Rate
kpis.description: Reflects the percentage of the HR budget that has been utilized during a given period. This metric gives an overview of how efficiently the HR department is operating in terms of financial resources, indicating whether they are under or over budget.
kpis.formula: (Amount of HR Budget Spent / Total HR Budget) x 100
kpis.example: If the HR department has spent $70,000 out of an allocated $100,000 budget, the HR Budget Utilization Rate would be 70%.

kpis.name: In-App Purchase Rate
kpis.description: In-App Purchase Rate calculates the percentage of app users who make purchases within the app. This metric is key for apps with a freemium model or those relying on in-app purchases for revenue. It indicates the effectiveness of monetization strategies within the app.
kpis.formula: (Number of Users Making In-App Purchases / Total App Users) * 100
kpis.example: If 50 out of 1,000 app users make an in-app purchase, the purchase rate is (50/1000)*100 = 5%.

kpis.name: In-App Purchase Revenue Growth
kpis.description: This metric tracks the growth rate of revenue generated from in-app purchases over time. It's crucial for understanding the financial success of monetization strategies within the app and user willingness to spend.
kpis.formula: ((Current Period Revenue - Previous Period Revenue) / Previous Period Revenue) * 100
kpis.example: If in-app purchase revenue increases from $1,000 to $1,500 over a month, the revenue growth is (($1500 - $1000) / $1000) * 100 = 50%.

kpis.name: Inactive Inventory as Percentage of Inventory Value
kpis.description: Measures the value of items that have not been sold or used for a long period, indicating potential dead stock that ties up capital.
kpis.formula: (Value of Inactive Inventory / Total Inventory Value) x 100
kpis.example: If the value of inactive stock is $50,000 out of a total inventory of $500,000, the rate is 10%.

kpis.name: Incident Count
kpis.description: The total number of incidents recorded in a given period. This metric helps in assessing the overall stability of the system and the frequency of disruptions faced.
kpis.formula: (Total Number of Incidents in a Period)
kpis.example: If 20 incidents are recorded in a month, the incident count is 20.

kpis.name: Incident Reoccurrence Rate
kpis.description: Calculates the frequency of repeated incidents, highlighting the effectiveness of measures taken to prevent similar future incidents.
kpis.formula: (Number of Repeated Incidents / Total Number of Incidents) * 100
kpis.example: If there are 5 repeated incidents out of 50 total, the reoccurrence rate is (5/50)*100 = 10%.

kpis.name: Incidents Related to Third-Party Breaches
kpis.description: Measures the number of security or compliance incidents that are directly linked to third-party actions or oversights. Third-party breaches can expose the organization to significant risks, including data loss, legal implications, and reputational damage.
kpis.formula: Count of incidents resulting from third-party breaches
kpis.example: If there were 4 incidents in a year related to third-party breaches, the KPI would be 4.

kpis.name: Industry Diversification
kpis.description: Measures the revenue spread across different industries or sectors. Having clients from diverse sectors can protect the consultancy from industry-specific downturns or disruptions.
kpis.formula: Revenue from a specific industry / Total revenue
kpis.example: If a consultancy earns $300,000 from the tech industry out of a $1 million total, the tech industry accounts for 30% of their revenue.

kpis.name: Infrastructure Cost Efficiency
kpis.description: Assesses how cost-effectively the infrastructure is utilized, balancing performance and reliability against cost.
kpis.formula: (Service Performance and Reliability / Infrastructure Cost)
kpis.example: If service reliability is valued at $10,000 and the infrastructure cost is $5,000, the efficiency is 10,000 / 5,000 = 2.

kpis.name: Infrastructure Costs
kpis.description: The total expenditure related to maintaining and upgrading the physical and digital infrastructure of a company. Ensuring efficient operations and keeping up with technological advancements can drive the company's competitive edge and scalability.
kpis.formula: Sum of all infrastructure-related expenses (hardware, software, networks, etc.)
kpis.example: If a company spends $50,000 on server maintenance, $25,000 on software subscriptions, and $10,000 on network security, the total infrastructure costs would be $85,000.

kpis.name: Insurance Costs as Percentage of Inventory Costs
kpis.description: Indicates the portion of inventory costs represented by insurance. Monitoring this can help in evaluating the cost-effectiveness of insurance strategies.
kpis.formula: (Insurance Costs for Inventory / Total Inventory Costs) x 100
kpis.example: If insurance costs for inventory are $2,000 and total inventory costs are $40,000, the percentage is 5%.

kpis.name: Internal Control Effectiveness Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of internal financial controls (checks and processes) that are effectively functioning as intended.  Effective controls minimize financial risks and ensure accurate reporting.
kpis.formula: (Number of effective controls / Total internal controls) x 100
kpis.example: If 45 out of 50 controls are deemed effective, this KPI is 90%.

kpis.name: Internal Project Rework Rate
kpis.description: The frequency with which project deliverables are revised before being presented to the client. High rework rates can indicate internal process inefficiencies, miscommunication, or gaps in skills. Minimizing rework can lead to faster project delivery and reduced costs.
kpis.formula: (Number of projects requiring rework / Total number of projects) x 100
kpis.example: If out of 100 projects, 15 needed internal revisions before presenting to the client, the rework rate would be 15%.

kpis.name: Internal Response Time
kpis.description: The average time taken for one support agent to respond to another agent's internal query or request related to an email case, in minutes. Efficient internal communication speeds up resolution times and ensures that customers aren't left waiting due to internal inefficiencies or lack of clarity.
kpis.formula: Total time taken for internal responses / Number of internal queries
kpis.example: If it took 120 minutes in total to respond to 24 internal queries, the average Internal Response Time is 5 minutes.

kpis.name: Inventory Accuracy
kpis.description: Measures the percentage comparison of the physical count of inventory items to the recorded amount in the inventory management system. Ensuring inventory accuracy prevents stockouts, reduces holding costs, and ensures timely deliveries to customers. A high accuracy rate indicates a well-managed inventory system.
kpis.formula: (Physical Countof Inventory / Recorded Inventory) x 100
kpis.example: If a warehouse's physical count shows 950 items but the system says 1000, the inventory accuracy is 95%.

kpis.name: Inventory Days of Supply
kpis.description: Indicates how many days the current inventory will last at the current sales rate without new stock. A critical metric for ensuring stock availability.
kpis.formula: (End Inventory / Cost of Goods Sold) * Days in Period
kpis.example: If the end inventory is $200,000, COGS is $1,000,000, and the period is 365 days, the days of supply is 73 days.

kpis.name: Inventory Holding Costs as Percentage of Gross Sales
kpis.description: This KPI measures the proportion of gross sales that's consumed by the costs of holding inventory. A higher percentage could indicate inefficiencies or high carrying costs.
kpis.formula: (Inventory Holding Costs / Gross Sales) x 100
kpis.example: If holding costs are $10,000 and gross sales are $200,000, the percentage is 5%.

kpis.name: Inventory Holding Costs as Percentage of Inventory Value
kpis.description: Represents the proportion of inventory value consumed by holding costs. A high percentage might signal that inventory management strategies need revisiting.
kpis.formula: (Inventory Holding Costs / Total Inventory Value) x 100
kpis.example: If holding costs are $10,000 and the total inventory value is $200,000, the percentage is 5%.

kpis.name: Inventory Location Accuracy
kpis.description: Determines how often inventory items are found in their designated locations within a warehouse or storage facility. Accurate location information increases warehouse efficiency, reduces picking time, minimizes order processing delays, and ensures smoother logistics operations.
kpis.formula: (Number of Items Correctly Located / Total Number of Items Checked) x 100
kpis.example: If 90 out of 100 checked items are in the correct spot, the location accuracy is 90%.

kpis.name: Inventory Shrinkage as Percentage of Inventory Value
kpis.description: Represents the reduction in the recorded inventory value that is not accounted for by sales. This includes theft, damage, miscounts, and other losses. Understanding shrinkage helps businesses adjust security measures, refine inventory processes, and improve profitability.
kpis.formula: (Value of Lost Inventory / Initial Inventory Value) x 100
kpis.example: If the initial inventory value was $50,000 and the value of lost inventory is $1,000, the shrinkage rate is 2%.

kpis.name: Inventory System Downtime
kpis.description: The amount of time the inventory management system is unavailable due to technical issues. Extended downtimes can disrupt operations and lead to revenue losses.
kpis.formula: Total Hours the Inventory System was Down in a Given Period
kpis.example: If the inventory system was down for 3 hours last month, the downtime is 3 hours.

kpis.name: Inventory to Obsolete Goods Ratio
kpis.description: Indicates the relation between the total inventory and obsolete items. A higher ratio can signal poor inventory management or changes in market demand.
kpis.formula: Total Inventory Value / Value of Obsolete Goods
kpis.example: If total inventory is worth $200,000 and obsolete goods are $10,000, the ratio is 20:1.

kpis.name: Inventory Turnover
kpis.description: This KPI measures how often an organization's inventory is sold and replaced over a given period. A high turnover indicates efficient inventory management and sales processes, while a low turnover might mean poor sales or excessive inventory.
kpis.formula: Cost of Goods Sold / Average Inventory
kpis.example: If the cost of goods sold is $1,000,000 and average inventory is $200,000, the inventory turnover is 5 times.

kpis.name: Inventory Turnover Ratio
kpis.description: Shows how often a company's inventory is sold and replaced over a period, highlighting the product's sales speed.
kpis.formula: Cost of Goods Sold / Average Inventory
kpis.example: If COGS is $1M and average inventory is $200k, turnover is $1M / $200k = 5 times.

kpis.name: Invite Conversion Rate
kpis.description: Invite Conversion Rate measures the success of referral or invitation programs by calculating the percentage of sent invites that result in new user registrations or sign-ups. It’s a key metric for assessing the effectiveness of word-of-mouth and referral marketing strategies. High conversion rates from invites are indicative of strong product advocacy among existing users.
kpis.formula: (Number of Registrations from Invites / Total Invites Sent) * 100
kpis.example: If 100 out of 500 sent invites lead to new user registrations, the Invite Conversion Rate is (100/500)*100 = 20%.

kpis.name: Involuntary Termination Rate
kpis.description:
The percentage of employees who were let go or terminated for reasons other than voluntary resignation or retirement during a specified time frame.

A high involuntary termination rate can indicate potential issues in recruitment processes, training, or workplace culture. It also affects morale and can have financial implications due to severance and replacement costs.
kpis.formula: (Number of involuntary terminations during the period / Average number of employees during the period) x 100
kpis.example: If a company with an average of 500 employees over the year had 10 involuntary terminations, the rate is 2%.

kpis.name: Issue Resolution Time
kpis.description: The speed at which project issues or problems are resolved can impact stakeholder satisfaction and project momentum. Faster resolution times can enhance stakeholder trust and confidence in the project management team.
kpis.formula: Issue Resolution Time = Total time taken to resolve an issue from the moment it's reported
kpis.example: If an issue is reported on Monday and it's resolved by Thursday, the issue resolution time is 3 days.

kpis.name: Job Leaving Ratio per Department
kpis.description: The percentage of employees who left their jobs within a specific department over a given time frame. This metric helps identify if there are department-specific issues affecting employee retention. High turnover in specific departments might indicate managerial problems, workload imbalances, or other departmental challenges.
kpis.formula: (Number of employees who left a department / Average number of employees in that department during the period) x 100
kpis.example: If the marketing department, averaging 50 employees over the year, sees 5 employees leave, the job leaving ratio for the department is 10%.

kpis.name: Job Offer Acceptance Rate
kpis.description: Showcases how many job offers extended by the company are accepted by candidates. A lower acceptance rate could indicate misaligned offers, uncompetitive packages, or a diminished company reputation. It helps HR refine their offer strategies to attract top talent.
kpis.formula: (Number of accepted offers / Total job offers made) x 100
kpis.example: If 70 out of 100 offers are accepted, the rate is 70%.

kpis.name: Joint Sales and Marketing Activities
kpis.description: Tracks the number of collaborative activities between sales and marketing teams, such as co-created campaigns, which can improve alignment and understanding.
kpis.formula: (Count of Joint Sales-Marketing Campaigns)
kpis.example: If there are 5 joint campaigns in a quarter, the joint activities number is 5.

kpis.name: Keyword Ranking
kpis.description: The position of your website on search engine results pages (SERPs) for specific keywords. Higher rankings increase the likelihood of attracting organic traffic.

kpis.name: Knowledge Retention Rate
kpis.description: Assesses the extent to which sales representatives retain knowledge from training over time, crucial for sustained sales effectiveness.
kpis.formula: (Number of Concepts Retained / Total Concepts Taught) * 100
kpis.example: If 20 concepts are taught and 18 are retained after a period, the retention rate is (18/20)*100 = 90%.

kpis.name: Knowledge Reuse Rate
kpis.description: Gauges how often previously developed solutions or knowledge artifacts are reused in new contexts or projects. Reusing knowledge can lead to efficiency gains, ensuring that consultants don't "reinvent the wheel" with every new project, and it also maintains consistency in service delivery.
kpis.formula: (Number of projects reusing previous solutions or knowledge / Total number of projects) x 100
kpis.example: If 20 out of 100 projects utilized previously developed solutions, the knowledge reuse rate is 20%.

kpis.name: Label Accuracy
kpis.description: Label Accuracy quantifies the correctness of the labels in the training dataset. Accurate labeling is crucial for supervised learning models to learn effectively.
kpis.formula: (Correctly Labeled Data / Total Data) * 100
kpis.example: If out of 1,000 labeled images, 980 are labeled correctly, label accuracy is (980/1000)*100 = 98%.

kpis.name: Lead Conversion Forecast
kpis.description: Forecasts the number of leads expected to convert into sales, essential for predicting revenue and assessing marketing efforts.
kpis.formula: Estimated based on historical conversion rates applied to current leads
kpis.example: With a 10% historical conversion rate and 200 new leads, the forecast is 200 * 0.10 = 20 conversions.

kpis.name: Lead Conversion Rate
kpis.description: Lead Conversion Rate tracks the percentage of leads that turn into customers, providing insight into sales funnel efficiency.
kpis.formula: (Number of Sales / Number of Leads) * 100
kpis.example: For 100 leads generating 25 sales, the conversion rate is (25/100)*100 = 25%.

kpis.name: Lead Drop-off Rate
kpis.description: Indicates the percentage of leads that exit the pipeline before converting to sales, highlighting potential issues in the sales funnel.
kpis.formula: (Number of Leads Lost / Total Number of Leads) * 100
kpis.example: If 15 out of 100 leads are lost, the drop-off rate is (15/100)*100 = 15%.

kpis.name: Lead Handoff SLA Compliance
kpis.description: Measures the adherence to service level agreements (SLAs) for the handoff of leads from marketing to sales, critical for a smooth transition and prompt action.
kpis.formula: (Number of Leads Handed Off Within SLA / Total Leads Handed Off) * 100
kpis.example: If 90 out of 100 leads are handed off within the agreed SLA, the compliance rate is (90/100)*100 = 90%.

kpis.name: Lead Response Time
kpis.description: The average time it takes for a sales rep to respond to a lead, showing the efficiency of the sales process.
kpis.formula: Sum of Response Times / Number of Leads
kpis.example: If the total response time for 10 leads is 500 minutes, the average response time is 500/10 = 50 minutes per lead.

kpis.name: Lead Scoring Average
kpis.description: The average score of leads based on their likelihood to convert. Allows sales and marketing teams to prioritize leads.
kpis.formula: Total score of all leads / Number of leads
kpis.example: If 10 leads have scores ranging from 10 to 90, the average score is 50.

kpis.name: Lead Time
kpis.description: Lead Time gauges the duration taken from the initiation of a project task to its completion, in hours or days. It's vital for predicting project timelines and managing stakeholder expectations.
kpis.formula: Lead Time = Date of task completion - Date of task initiation
kpis.example: If a task was started on January 1st and finished on January 10th, the lead time is 10 days.

kpis.name: Lead Time
kpis.description: Tracks the total duration from the inception of an idea to its deployment in production. This KPI is crucial for understanding the speed and efficiency of the development cycle, highlighting areas for process optimization.
kpis.formula: (Time from Idea to Deployment)
kpis.example: If a feature takes 40 days from conception to being deployed, the lead time is 40 days.

kpis.name: Leadership Transition Success Rate
kpis.description: Measures how successfully leadership transitions occur, often based on metrics like the performance of the successor or team/department outcomes. Smooth leadership transitions are pivotal for organizational continuity and maintaining stakeholder confidence.
kpis.formula: (Number of Successful Leadership Transitions / Total Leadership Transitions) x 100
kpis.example: If 9 out of 10 leadership transitions are deemed successful, the rate is 90%.

kpis.name: Leads Generated At Event
kpis.description: The number of potential clients or customers identified at the event. Measures the direct marketing success of the event.

kpis.name: Leads Generated via Organic Search
kpis.description: Represents the percentage of total leads that are sourced from organic search. Helps to quantify the effectiveness of organic search strategies in driving potential customers.
kpis.formula: (Number of Leads from Organic Search / Total Number of Leads) x 100
kpis.example: If you generated 200 leads in a month and 50 of them came from organic search, then the percentage is 25%.

kpis.name: Learning Sessions Held for Top Management
kpis.description:
The number of dedicated learning or training sessions specifically tailored for the organization's top management or leadership team.

Even experienced leaders benefit from continual learning. Regular sessions for top management signal a commitment to leadership development, keeping executives updated on best practices, industry trends, and leadership techniques.
kpis.formula: Number of sessions held for top management within a given period
kpis.example: If there are 10 leadership training sessions conducted for top executives in a year, the count is 10.

kpis.name: Legal Fees and Settlements
kpis.description: The total costs related to legal consultations, proceedings, and settlements. Provides a perspective on potential legal challenges and their financial impact.
kpis.formula: Sum of all legal-related fees and settlement costs
kpis.example: If a company pays $20,000 in legal consultations and $100,000 in settlements, the total would be $120,000.

kpis.name: License and Royalty Costs
kpis.description: The total amount paid for licenses (e.g., software) and royalties (payments made to stakeholders for using their assets). Keeping track helps ensure budgetary compliance and understand the cost of partnerships or asset usage.
kpis.formula: Sum of all license fees + royalties paid
kpis.example: If a company pays $10,000 for software licenses and $5,000 in royalty fees, the total would be $15,000.

kpis.name: Lifetime Value of an Account
kpis.description: Predicts the total revenue a business can expect from a single account throughout the business relationship.
kpis.formula: Average Purchase Value * Purchase Frequency * Customer Lifespan
kpis.example: An account with a $100 average purchase, 10 purchases/year, and 5-year lifespan has a LTV of $100 * 10 * 5 = $5,000.

kpis.name: Loyalty/Repeat Customer Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of customers who make repeat purchases or stay loyal to the brand. Highlights the success of post-purchase engagement and satisfaction efforts.
kpis.formula: (Number of repeat customers / Total number of customers) x 100
kpis.example: If 100 customers result in 20 making another purchase, the loyalty rate is 20%.

kpis.name: Market Growth Rate Forecast
kpis.description: Estimates the expected growth rate of the market, providing insight for potential sales growth and strategic positioning.
kpis.formula: ((Forecasted Market Volume - Current Market Volume) / Current Market Volume) * 100
kpis.example: If the current market volume is $1M and the forecast for the next year is $1.1M, the growth rate forecast is (($1.1M - $1M) / $1M) * 100 = 10%.

kpis.name: Market Share Percentage
kpis.description: Indicates the company's portion of total sales in a specific market, used to gauge competitiveness and market control.
kpis.formula: (Company's Total Sales in Market / Total Market Sales) * 100
kpis.example: If a company has $1 million in sales in a market with $10 million in sales, market share is ($1M / $10M) * 100 = 10%.

kpis.name: Marketing and Advertising Expenditure
kpis.description: The overall costs associated with promoting products or services. Helps determine the ROI of marketing efforts and how they contribute to sales and brand recognition.
kpis.formula: Sum of all marketing and advertising-related expenses
kpis.example: If a company spends $50,000 on digital ads and $10,000 on print media, the total expenditure is $60,000.

kpis.name: Marketing Contribution to Closed Deals
kpis.description: Quantifies marketing's contribution to the total number of closed deals, demonstrating the effectiveness of marketing efforts in contributing to sales success.
kpis.formula: (Closed Deals with Marketing Contribution / Total Closed Deals) * 100
kpis.example: If marketing contributed to 40 out of 200 closed deals, the contribution percentage is (40/200)*100 = 20%.

kpis.name: Marketing Influence on Sales Pipeline
kpis.description: Calculates the percentage of sales pipeline opportunities that are influenced by marketing activities, showcasing the impact of marketing on the sales pipeline.
kpis.formula: (Number of Opportunities Influenced by Marketing / Total Opportunities) * 100
kpis.example: If marketing influences 200 out of 1000 opportunities, the influence rate is (200/1000)*100 = 20%.

kpis.name: Marketing Qualified Leads (MQLs) to Sales Accepted Leads (SALs)
kpis.description: Tracks the rate at which marketing qualified leads are accepted by the sales team, indicative of the quality of leads and agreement on lead qualification standards.
kpis.formula: (Number of MQLs Accepted as SALs / Total MQLs)
kpis.example: If 80 out of 100 MQLs are accepted by sales, the MQLs to SALs ratio is (80/100) = 0.8.

kpis.name: Marketing Time per Episode
kpis.description: The amount of time devoted to marketing activities specifically for each episode. Efficient marketing ensures greater reach and potential for monetization.

kpis.name: Mean Time to Acknowledge
kpis.description: Assesses the average time taken for a team to acknowledge an incident after detection. This metric indicates the team's readiness and promptness in initiating a response to incidents.
kpis.formula: (Total Time Taken to Acknowledge All Incidents / Total Number of Incidents)
kpis.example: For 5 incidents acknowledged in 50 minutes, the mean time to acknowledge is 50/5 = 10 minutes per incident.

kpis.name: Mean Time to Detect
kpis.description: Measures the average time taken to detect an incident after it has occurred, indicating the effectiveness of monitoring and alerting systems. A shorter time to detect suggests a more responsive incident detection process.
kpis.formula: (Total Time Taken to Detect All Incidents / Total Number of Incidents)
kpis.example: If it takes 90 minutes to detect 3 incidents, the mean time to detect is 90/3 = 30 minutes per incident.

kpis.name: Mean Time to Detect Incidents
kpis.description: Measures the average time it takes to detect an incident after it has occurred. Faster detection allows quicker response, reducing potential impact.
kpis.formula: Total time to detect all incidents / Number of incidents
kpis.example: If 4 incidents took a total of 80 minutes to detect, the mean time would be 20 minutes.

kpis.name: Mean Time to Recover
kpis.description: Reflects the average time required to recover from a failure in the production environment. This metric is indicative of the team's capability to promptly resolve issues and restore normal service, minimizing downtime.
kpis.formula: (Total Downtime / Number of Failures)
kpis.example: For instance, if the team encounters 3 hours of total downtime over 6 failures, the mean time to recover is 3/6 = 0.5 hours per failure.

kpis.name: Mean Time to Respond to Incidents
kpis.description: This KPI measures the average time taken to respond to an incident once it's detected. Quick response times can mitigate damage and prevent further risks.
kpis.formula: Total time to respond to all incidents / Number of incidents
kpis.example: If 4 incidents took a total of 160 minutes to respond to, the mean time would be 40 minutes.

kpis.name: Media Mentions
kpis.description: The number of times a brand, product, or company representative is mentioned in the media. It's an indicator of the brand's visibility and reach in media channels.

kpis.name: Medical Leave Frequency
kpis.description: Tracks how often employees take medical leave. An increasing trend in medical leave can indicate potential health issues among staff or problems with the working environment. It’s essential to address these for employee well-being and to maintain operational efficiency.
kpis.formula: Total Number of Medical Leaves Taken in a Given Period
kpis.example: If 20 medical leaves were taken in a month across the company, the value for this KPI would be 20.

kpis.name: Meetups Organized
kpis.description: Counts the number of meetups or community events organized around the project. This metric is indicative of community-building efforts and engagement beyond code contributions.
kpis.formula: Total Number of Meetups or Events
kpis.example: If the community organizes 15 meetups in a year, the count is 15.

kpis.name: Memory Usage
kpis.description: Amount of memory used by the application during execution. Efficient memory usage is crucial for performance and scalability.
kpis.formula: Average Memory Used During Execution
kpis.example: If an application uses an average of 120MB of memory, the memory usage is 120MB.

kpis.name: Memory Usage
kpis.description: Indicates the amount of memory used by the application during execution. Efficient memory usage is key to performance and scalability.
kpis.formula: Average Memory Used During Execution
kpis.example: If an application uses an average of 120MB of memory, the memory usage is 120MB.

kpis.name: Missed Chats
kpis.description: The number of chat sessions initiated by customers that received no response from an agent. Missed chats can lead to lost sales opportunities and decreased customer satisfaction. It's crucial to ensure that chat requests are promptly addressed.
kpis.formula: Total number of chats with no agent response
kpis.example: If 7 chat sessions went unanswered on a given day, Missed Chats for that day is 7.

kpis.name: Missed Emails
kpis.description: The number of customer emails that were overlooked or not responded to. Missed emails can lead to customer dissatisfaction and potential churn. Tracking this KPI ensures that all customer queries are addressed.
kpis.formula: Total number of missed emails
kpis.example: If 3 emails from customers were overlooked in a week, then Missed Emails for that week is 3.

kpis.name: Mobile Retention Rate
kpis.description: Mobile Retention Rate measures the proportion of users who continue to use the app over a specific time frame after installation. This KPI is vital for assessing the app's ability to retain users and its long-term appeal. High retention rates are often indicative of a valuable and engaging app experience.
kpis.formula: (Number of Users Remaining After a Set Time / Number of Users at Start of Period) * 100
kpis.example: If 800 out of 1,000 users who downloaded an app are still using it after a month, the retention rate is (800/1000)*100 = 80%.

kpis.name: Mobile Traffic
kpis.description: The number or percentage of users accessing your site from mobile devices. As mobile internet use grows, ensuring a strong mobile user experience is crucial.
kpis.formula: (Mobile visitors / Total visitors) x 100
kpis.example: If 1,200 out of 2,000 total visitors came from mobile, the mobile traffic percentage is 60%.

kpis.name: Model Accuracy Rate
kpis.description: This metric assesses the overall accuracy of an AI model, indicating the percentage of total predictions made correctly, both positives and negatives.
kpis.formula: (True Positives + True Negatives) / Total Predictions
kpis.example: If a model correctly predicts 90 out of 100 observations (including both positives and negatives), its accuracy rate is 90%.

kpis.name: Model F1 Score
kpis.description: The F1 Score is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, providing a balance between them. It’s used when an equilibrium between Precision and Recall is desired.
kpis.formula: 2 * ((Precision * Recall) / (Precision + Recall))
kpis.example: If the precision is 80% and recall is 60%, the F1 score is 2*((0.8*0.6)/(0.8+0.6)) = 0.69.

kpis.name: Model Failure Rate
kpis.description: The frequency at which the AI model fails to provide a valid output or encounters errors during operation. A lower failure rate indicates higher reliability and stability.
kpis.formula: (Number of Failures / Total Model Uses) * 100
kpis.example: If a model fails 5 times out of 1,000 uses, the failure rate is (5/1000)*100 = 0.5%.

kpis.name: Model Interpretability Index
kpis.description: This index assesses how understandable the model’s decisions or predictions are to humans. High interpretability is crucial for trust and transparency, especially in sensitive applications.
kpis.formula: Interpretability Assessment Methodology
kpis.example: A model that provides clear explanations for its predictions, such as decision trees, might score 85/100 on interpretability.

kpis.name: Model Precision
kpis.description: Model Precision measures the accuracy of positive predictions made by an AI model. It’s crucial for scenarios where false positives have significant consequences. High precision indicates a lower rate of false positives.
kpis.formula: (True Positives / (True Positives + False Positives))
kpis.example: If an AI model identifies 80 relevant items (true positives) out of 100 total identified items, where 20 are irrelevant (false positives), precision is (80/(80+20)) = 80%.

kpis.name: Model Recall
kpis.description: Model Recall, or Sensitivity, calculates the proportion of actual positives correctly identified. It’s important in cases where missing any positive is critical, like disease detection.
kpis.formula: (True Positives / (True Positives + False Negatives))
kpis.example: In a disease detection model, if there are 50 actual cases of disease (true positives) and the model identifies 40, missing 10 (false negatives), recall is (40/(40+10)) = 80%.

kpis.name: Model Robustness Score
kpis.description: Model Robustness Score measures an AI model's ability to maintain performance when exposed to new, unseen data or adversarial conditions. It's important for assessing how well the model generalizes and performs under varied or unexpected inputs.
kpis.formula: Robustness Testing Methodology
kpis.example: If an AI model maintains 90% of its accuracy on a new dataset designed to test its limits, it may be given a robustness score of 90/100.

kpis.name: Model Scalability Rate
kpis.description: Evaluates how well an AI model maintains its performance as the amount of data increases. It’s key for understanding the model’s capability to handle larger datasets.
kpis.formula: Performance Measure at Scale

kpis.name: Model Update Frequency
kpis.description: Measures how often an AI model is updated or retrained. Regular updates can indicate a model that stays current with new data and trends.
kpis.formula: (Number of Updates / Time Period)
kpis.example: If a model is updated monthly, the update frequency is once per month.
