kpis.name: Sell-through Rate
kpis.description: Measures the amount of inventory sold in relation to the amount received. It provides insights into sales velocity and inventory effectiveness.
kpis.formula: (Units Sold / Units Received) x 100
kpis.example: If 80 units are sold out of 100 received, the sell-through rate is 80%.

kpis.name: Sentiment Analysis
kpis.description: Measurement of the tone (positive, neutral, negative) of media mentions. Assesses public and media perception of the brand.

kpis.name: Service Level Indicators
kpis.description: Service Level Indicators (SLIs) are specific, quantifiable measures of service reliability, such as uptime, error rates, or response times. These indicators are used to define the expected performance and reliability of a service.
kpis.formula: (Measured Value / Expected Value) * 100
kpis.example: If the expected uptime is 99.9% and the measured uptime is 99.5%, the SLI for uptime is (99.5 / 99.9) * 100 = 99.6%.

kpis.name: Service Level Objectives
kpis.description: Service Level Objectives (SLOs) are targets for Service Level Indicators (SLIs), representing the desired level of service reliability. They are crucial for aligning reliability goals with business objectives and customer expectations.
kpis.formula: (Achieved SLI Value / SLO Target) * 100
kpis.example: If the SLO target for uptime is 99.9% and the achieved uptime SLI is 99.5%, the SLO achievement is (99.5 / 99.9) * 100 = 99.6%.

kpis.name: Service Line Diversification
kpis.description: Looks at how revenue is distributed across various service offerings of the consultancy. Diversifying service offerings can provide stability. If one service line experiences a downturn, others might compensate.
kpis.formula: Revenue from a specific service line / Total revenue
kpis.example: If a consultancy's software development service generates $400,000 out of a $1 million total, this service line accounts for 40% of the revenue.

kpis.name: Session Depth
kpis.description: Session Depth quantifies the average number of interactions (such as clicks or screen views) a user has in a single session. This metric indicates user engagement intensity and content exploration depth. Higher session depth suggests that users are more actively exploring and using different features of the product.
kpis.formula: Average Number of Interactions or Pages Per Session
kpis.example: If users typically interact with 5 different screens per session in an app, the average Session Depth is 5.

kpis.name: Session Interval
kpis.description: Session Interval measures the average time between individual user sessions, offering insights into user habit formation and product reliance. Shorter intervals suggest higher user reliance on the product, possibly indicating its integral role in the user's routine or workflow.
kpis.formula: Average Time Between Sessions
kpis.example: If users generally return to an app every 2 days, the average Session Interval is 2 days.

kpis.name: Session Length
kpis.description: Session Length is the average duration a user spends in a single session when interacting with the software. It provides insights into user engagement depth and content or feature absorption. Longer sessions may indicate higher user interest and engagement with the product, whereas shorter sessions might suggest a need for more compelling or relevant content.
kpis.formula: Average Duration of User Sessions
kpis.example: If users typically spend 10 minutes per session on a software, the average Session Length is 10 minutes.

kpis.name: Severity of Incidents
kpis.description: Categorizes incidents based on their severity levels, such as critical, high, medium, and low. This helps in prioritizing incident response and understanding the impact on the system.
kpis.formula: Classification of Each Incident
kpis.example: For example, out of 10 incidents, 2 are critical, 3 are high, 4 are medium, and 1 is low severity.

kpis.name: Share of Voice
kpis.description: The percentage of media mentions a brand receives compared to its competitors. Shows how much a brand dominates the conversation in its industry.
kpis.formula: (Brand's Media Mentions / Total Industry Media Mentions) x 100
kpis.example: If a brand has 50 media mentions and its industry as a whole has 500 mentions, its share of voice is 10%.

kpis.name: Shares per Video
kpis.description: The number of times the video has been shared across social platforms or through other mediums. Shows the video's shareability and organic reach potential.

kpis.name: Size of Safety Stock
kpis.description: Safety stock is the quantity of stock held to guard against stockouts. It acts as a buffer against demand variability and supply chain disruptions.
kpis.formula: Total Number of Units Held as Safety Stock
kpis.example: If an organization holds 500 units of a product as a buffer, the size of the safety stock is 500 units.

kpis.name: Slogan Recall Rate
kpis.description: Rate of surveyed individuals who can recall your brand's slogan or tagline. A memorable slogan can reinforce brand values, boost recall, and help in differentiating your brand from competitors.
kpis.formula: (Number of people who recall the slogan / Total number of people surveyed) x 100
kpis.example: If 150 out of 500 surveyed individuals can recall your brand slogan, your Slogan Recall Rate is 30%.

kpis.name: Social Conversion Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of social media users who take a desired action, such as signing up for a newsletter or making a purchase, after interacting with a post or ad. It's vital to understand not just if your content is seen, but if it drives action.
kpis.formula: (Number of Desired Actions / Number of Clicks) x 100
kpis.example: If 100 users click through to your landing page and 10 make a purchase, the conversion rate is 10%.

kpis.name: Social Media Impressions
kpis.description: Counts the number of times content has been displayed on someone's screen. Note that this doesn’t mean it was clicked, just that it was displayed. Impressions give you an idea of the potential reach of your content. A high number of impressions suggests that your content has a wide audience, but it doesn't necessarily mean high engagement.

kpis.name: Social Response Rate and Time
kpis.description: Measures how frequently the brand responds to audience engagements (like comments or direct messages) and how quickly. A fast and high response rate can improve brand reputation and customer satisfaction.
kpis.formula:
For Rate: (Number of Responses / Number of Engagements) x 100.

For Time: Average time taken to respond.
kpis.example: If you responded to 80 out of 100 comments, your response rate is 80%. If your average response time is 30 minutes, it indicates a fairly rapid engagement with your audience.

kpis.name: Social Share of Voice
kpis.description: Measures your brand mentions relative to your competitors. Allows companies to understand their market position in the context of social chatter.
kpis.formula: (Your Brand's Mentions / Total Market Mentions) x 100
kpis.example: If your brand is mentioned 50 times and the total mentions in your industry are 500, your SSoV is 10%.

kpis.name: Social Shares per Episode
kpis.description: The number of times an episode has been shared across various social media platforms. Indicates the virality and shareworthiness of your content.

kpis.name: Social Shares per Post
kpis.description: The average number of times a blog post is shared on social media platforms. Indicates the virality and share-worthiness of the content.
kpis.formula: Total social shares for all posts / Number of posts
kpis.example: If 10 posts get shared 500 times in total, the average social shares per post is 50.

kpis.name: Software Development Cost Variance
kpis.description: The difference between the budgeted cost for software development and the actual cost incurred. Indicates how well the company is managing its software development expenses in comparison to what was budgeted.
kpis.formula: Budgeted Cost for Software Development − Actual Cost for Software Development
kpis.example: If a company budgeted $2 million for a software project but ended up spending $2.5 million, the variance would be -$500,000.

kpis.name: Stakeholder Engagement Rate
kpis.description: Quantifies how actively involved stakeholders are in the project. Active engagement is vital for alignment, feedback, and overall project success.
kpis.formula: Stakeholder Engagement Rate = (Number of engaged stakeholders / Total stakeholders) x 100
kpis.example: If out of 20 stakeholders, 18 are actively participating and providing feedback, the engagement rate is 90%.

kpis.name: Stakeholder Satisfaction Survey Score
kpis.description: This score quantifies the satisfaction of stakeholders, including sponsors, team members, and customers. Consistently low scores can indicate misalignment between project deliverables and stakeholder expectations.
kpis.formula: Measured through regular surveys where stakeholders rate their satisfaction on a predetermined scale.
kpis.example: After a project milestone, stakeholders rate their satisfaction on a scale from 1-5. An average score of 4.2 suggests a high level of stakeholder satisfaction.

kpis.name: Stickiness Ratio
kpis.description: The Stickiness Ratio compares the daily and monthly active users (DAU/MAU) to gauge how often users return to the software. A higher ratio suggests that users are more regularly engaged with the product, indicating addictive or habit-forming product qualities. This metric is vital for products relying on frequent user interaction.
kpis.formula: (DAU / MAU) * 100
kpis.example: With a DAU of 5,000 and an MAU of 20,000, the Stickiness Ratio is (5000/20000)*100 = 25%.

kpis.name: Stock Availability Rate
kpis.description: Indicates the percentage of time items are available in stock when requested, pointing to the effectiveness of inventory management.
kpis.formula: (Number of Times Stock is Available / Total Requests) x 100
kpis.example: If stock is available 180 times out of 200 requests, the availability rate is 90%.

kpis.name: Stock Cover
kpis.description: The duration of time the current inventory will last given the sales rate. It's vital to know how long stock will last without replenishment.
kpis.formula: Average Inventory / Daily Sales
kpis.example: If you have 1460 units of stock and sell 10 units/day, the stock cover is 146 days.

kpis.name: Stock Obsolescence Rate
kpis.description: Shows the rate at which inventory becomes obsolete. Rising rates can indicate changing market demands or issues in inventory management.
kpis.formula: (Number of Obsolete Stock Items / Total Stock Items) x 100
kpis.example: If 100 out of 5000 items become obsolete, the rate is 2%.

kpis.name: Stockouts in Period
kpis.description: The number of times stock was unavailable when requested in a specific period. Frequent stockouts can lead to lost sales and decreased customer satisfaction.
kpis.formula: Total Stockouts in Period
kpis.example: If stock was unavailable 10 times in a month, there were 10 stockouts.

kpis.name: Subscriber Growth Rate
kpis.description: The percentage growth in the number of subscribers to a video channel over a specific period, compared to the previous period. Measures the pace at which a channel is gaining subscribers, indicating its momentum and the effectiveness of its content in attracting viewers.
kpis.formula: [(Number of Subscribers at the End of Period - Number of Subscribers at the Start of Period) / Number of Subscribers at the Start of Period] x 100
kpis.example: If a channel starts the month with 50,000 subscribers and ends with 55,000, the subscriber growth rate for that month is 10%.

kpis.name: Subscriber List Growth
kpis.description: The rate at which your email newsletter subscriber list is growing. Indicates the effectiveness of your subscriber acquisition efforts and overall interest in your content.
kpis.formula: [(New Subscribers - Unsubscribes) / Total Number of Email Addresses on Your List] x 100
kpis.example: If you gain 100 new subscribers and lose 20, on a list of 1,000, the growth rate is 8%.

kpis.name: Succession Readiness Rate
kpis.description: Measures the proportion of critical roles that have identified successors who are ready to take over immediately if needed. Ensuring seamless transitions in critical roles minimizes business disruption and supports strategic continuity.
kpis.formula: (Number of Critical Roles with Immediate Successors / Total Critical Roles) x 100
kpis.example: If there are 10 critical roles and 7 have immediate successors, the Succession Readiness Rate would be 70%.

kpis.name: Supplier Compliance Rate
kpis.description: Measures how frequently a supplier adheres to contractual terms, industry standards, and regulatory guidelines. Ensuring compliance is pivotal to prevent potential legal issues and safeguard the company's reputation.
kpis.formula: (Number of Compliant Supplier Deliveries / Total Supplier Deliveries) x 100
kpis.example: If a supplier adhered to compliance terms for 95 out of 100 deliveries, the compliance rate is 95%.

kpis.name: Supplier Defect Rate
kpis.description: Represents the percentage of received goods from a supplier that are defective. A low defect rate is crucial for maintaining quality standards and reducing return and rework costs.
kpis.formula: (Number of Defective Items Received / Total Number of Items Received) x 100
kpis.example: If 5 out of 1,000 items received from a supplier are defective, the defect rate is 0.5%.

kpis.name: Supplier Lead Time Variability
kpis.description: Indicates the variation in lead time for orders from a particular supplier. High variability can make inventory planning challenging and may necessitate higher safety stock levels.
kpis.formula: Standard Deviation of Supplier Lead Times
kpis.example: If the lead times over the past month were 5, 6, 7, 5, and 8 days, the standard deviation might be 1.2 days, indicating variability.

kpis.name: Supplier On-time Delivery Rate
kpis.description: Measures the frequency at which suppliers deliver orders within the stipulated timeframe. A high rate ensures the supply chain runs smoothly and prevents potential stockouts.
kpis.formula: (Number of On-Time Deliveries from Supplier / Total Number of Deliveries from Supplier) x 100
kpis.example: If a supplier delivered 90 times on-time out of 100 deliveries, the on-time delivery rate is 90%.

kpis.name: System Changes Made Without Documentation Updates
kpis.description: Tracks the number of changes made to systems or processes without a corresponding update in the documentation. Making changes without updating documentation can lead to miscommunication, errors, and non-compliance.
kpis.formula: Count of system changes without corresponding documentation updates
kpis.example: If 5 system changes were made without updating the documentation in a year, the KPI would be 5.

kpis.name: System Usability Scale
kpis.description: The System Usability Scale (SUS) is a reliable tool for measuring the usability of a product. It provides a quick, global view of subjective assessments of usability, covering a variety of aspects from effectiveness to efficiency and satisfaction.
kpis.formula: SUS Questionnaire Scoring
kpis.example: SUS scores range from 0 to 100, with higher scores indicating better usability. An SUS score above 68 is considered above average.

kpis.name: Task Success Rate
kpis.description: Task Success Rate measures the percentage of successfully completed tasks by users. It reflects the effectiveness and intuitiveness of the product’s design. A high rate indicates that users can easily accomplish their goals, signifying good usability.
kpis.formula: (Number of Successful Tasks / Total Tasks Attempted) * 100
kpis.example: If users attempt 100 tasks and successfully complete 90, the Task Success Rate is (90/100)*100 = 90%.

kpis.name: Tax Audit Pass Rate
kpis.description: Measures the percentage of tax audits passed without additional liabilities or penalties. A high pass rate indicates that the company's tax practices and documentation are in compliance with regulations. A lower rate might signal a need for improved internal controls.
kpis.formula: (Number of Audits Passed / Total Audits Conducted) x 100
kpis.example: If a company underwent 10 tax audits in a year and passed 9 of them without additional liabilities, the Tax Audit Pass Rate would be 90%.

kpis.name: Tax Savings Rate
kpis.description: Represents the percentage of potential taxes saved due to deductions, credits, or other tax strategies. A high savings rate indicates effective tax planning, which can significantly impact a company's bottom line.
kpis.formula: (Potential Taxes - Actual Taxes Paid) / Potential Taxes x 100
kpis.example: If a company had a potential tax of $200,000 but, due to various deductions and credits, paid only $160,000, the Tax Savings Rate would be 20%.

kpis.name: Team Mood
kpis.description: Team mood is an intangible yet crucial indicator of overall team satisfaction and motivation. It can help project managers address potential issues before they escalate. A consistently low score can indicate deeper underlying issues, possibly affecting project outcomes.
kpis.formula: Typically measured through regular surveys or feedback tools, where team members rate their current mood on a predetermined scale.
kpis.example: In a weekly check-in, team members rate their mood with one of three emojis (positive, negative, neutral) or on a scale 1-5 or 1-10. An average score of 8 suggests a generally positive team mood.

kpis.name: Team Turnover
kpis.description: Team turnover rate reflects how often team members leave during the project's lifespan. High turnover can disrupt project momentum, increase costs, and indicate deeper issues such as dissatisfaction or poor management.
kpis.formula: Team Turnover = (Number of team members who left during the project / Average number of team members during the project) x 100
kpis.example: If 5 team members leave during a project, with an average team size of 50, the team turnover rate is 10%.

kpis.name: Team Velocity
kpis.description: Measures the amount of work a team completes in a sprint or iteration, typically in story points or number of features. It's a key indicator of team productivity and efficiency.
kpis.formula: (Total Story Points or Features Completed / Sprint Duration)
kpis.example: If a team completes 50 story points in a two-week sprint, the team velocity is 50 story points per sprint.

kpis.name: Technical Performance Measures
kpis.description: These are specific technical metrics used to gauge the performance of a deliverable against its requirements. It ensures the technical integrity of the project.
kpis.formula: Specific to the technical aspect being measured
kpis.example: For a software application, it might be 'response time under load' or 'maximum number of concurrent users'.

kpis.name: Territory Coverage Ratio
kpis.description: Reflects how well sales resources are distributed across a territory, based on the number of sales reps to potential customer ratio.
kpis.formula: Number of Sales Reps / Number of Potential Customers
kpis.example: With 10 reps for 2000 potential customers, coverage ratio is 10 / 2000 = 0.005 reps per customer.

kpis.name: Territory Penetration Rate
kpis.description: Shows the percentage of potential customers in a territory that have been converted to actual customers, indicating market penetration.
kpis.formula: (Number of Customers in Territory / Total Potential Customers in Territory) * 100
kpis.example: If there are 200 actual customers out of 1000 potential ones, penetration rate is (200 / 1000) * 100 = 20%.

kpis.name: Territory Profitability Index
kpis.description: Analyzes profitability by comparing revenue to costs within a territory, indicating financial success.
kpis.formula: Revenue from Territory / Costs to Serve Territory
kpis.example: If revenue is $100,000 and costs are $80,000, the profitability index is $100,000 / $80,000 = 1.25.

kpis.name: Territory Quota Attainment Rate
kpis.description: Measures the percentage of sales quota achieved in a specific territory, assessing sales rep performance.
kpis.formula: (Actual Sales in Territory / Sales Quota for Territory) * 100
kpis.example: If the sales quota is $100,000 and actual sales are $90,000, attainment is ($90,000 / $100,000) * 100 = 90%.

kpis.name: Test Pass / Failure Rate
kpis.description: Measures the percentage of tests that pass during the development process. A high failure rate can indicate issues with the code or the tests themselves.
kpis.formula: (Number of Passed Tests / Total Tests Conducted) * 100
kpis.example: If 90 out of 100 tests pass, the pass rate is (90/100)*100 = 90%.

kpis.name: Third Parties Compliant with Desired Standards
kpis.description: The percentage of third parties (like suppliers, vendors, or partners) that meet the organization's compliance standards, such as ISO 27001 or SOC 2. Ensuring third parties are compliant helps reduce risk and ensures that the company's compliance standards are upheld throughout the supply chain.
kpis.formula: (Number of third parties that meet the required standards / Total number of third parties) x 100
kpis.example: If 36 out of 40 third parties adhere to the company's desired compliance standards, the KPI would be 90%.

kpis.name: Time On Task
kpis.description: Time On Task tracks the average duration taken by users to complete a specific task. It indicates the efficiency of the product's design in enabling users to accomplish their goals. Longer times may suggest usability issues or a need for more streamlined workflows.
kpis.formula: Average Time Taken to Complete a Task
kpis.example: If users take an average of 5 minutes to find and use a feature, the Time On Task is 5 minutes.

kpis.name: Time Required for Creation of Promo Materials
kpis.description: The amount of time spent on creating promotional materials like clips, ads, banners, or posts for each episode. Marketing each episode efficiently maximizes reach and audience engagement.

kpis.name: Time Required for Creation of Promo Materials
kpis.description: The duration spent creating promotional content like trailers, teasers, and snippets for the video. Gives insight into promotional efforts and time investment in marketing the video.

kpis.name: Time Required for Post-Production per Episode
kpis.description: The number of hours spent on production and post-production editing for each podcast episode. Efficient editing ensures a high-quality episode, but overly long editing time can increase production costs.

kpis.name: Time Required for Post-Production per Video
kpis.description: The time dedicated to post-production tasks like editing, adding effects, sound mixing, etc., for a video. Assists in budgeting time and understanding the complexity of the post-production process.

kpis.name: Time Spent on Technical Debt
kpis.description: Tracks the amount of time spent addressing technical debt, which includes refactoring code, improving design, or updating documentation, crucial for long-term project health.
kpis.formula: Total Time Spent on Technical Debt Activities
kpis.example: If a team spends 40 hours in a month on technical debt, the time spent is 40 hours.

kpis.name: Time Spent on Technical Debt
kpis.description: Tracks the time dedicated to addressing technical debt, including code refactoring and design improvement, essential for long-term project health.
kpis.formula: Total Time Spent on Technical Debt Activities
kpis.example: If a team spends 40 hours in a month on technical debt, the time spent is 40 hours.

kpis.name: Time Taken to Close Audit Findings
kpis.description: Measures the average time it takes to address and resolve issues identified during audits, in days. Prolonged resolution times may indicate systemic issues, inefficiencies, or lack of resources dedicated to compliance.
kpis.formula: Total days taken to close all findings / Number of findings
kpis.example: If it took 300 days to close 10 audit findings, the average time is 30 days per finding.

kpis.name: Time Taken to Onboard a Third-Party
kpis.description: The average time it takes to do due dilligence and onboard a new third party from initial contact to full integration. A streamlined onboarding process ensures that third parties can quickly align with organizational processes and compliance requirements.
kpis.formula: Total time taken for all third-party onboardings / Number of third parties onboarded
kpis.example: If 5 third parties took a total of 50 days to onboard, the average time would be 10 days.

kpis.name: Time Taken to Update Documentation Post Process Changes
kpis.description: The average time taken to update existing documentation after changes in processes. Swift documentation updates ensure that employees always have access to the most recent and relevant information.
kpis.formula: Total time taken for all documentation updates / Number of updates
kpis.example: If 10 documentation updates took a total of 40 days, the average time would be 4 days per update.

kpis.name: Time to Acquire New Clients
kpis.description: The duration it takes from the point the consultancy recognizes the need to acquire a new client (active pursuit) until they successfully secure a new client agreement. In situations where client referrals aren't generating new business, this metric helps gauge the efficiency of the consultancy's proactive business development efforts. Shorter durations can mean effective outreach, networking, and pitch strategies, ensuring better consultant utilization and stable revenues.
kpis.formula: Date of securing new client agreement - Date of decision to actively pursue new clients
kpis.example: If a consultancy recognized the need to actively pursue new clients on April 1st and secured a new client agreement on April 25th, the Time to Acquire New Clients is 24 days.

kpis.name: Time to Answer Employee Suggestions
kpis.description: Average number of days it takes the HR or management team to respond to employee suggestions or feedback. Rapid responses show that the organization values employee input. Delays or lack of feedback can erode trust and discourage employees from offering potentially valuable insights in the future.
kpis.formula: Total number of days taken to respond to all suggestions / Total number of suggestions received
kpis.example: If it takes 150 days in total to respond to 50 suggestions, the average response time is 3 days.

kpis.name: Time to Bid Preparation
kpis.description: Reflects the average time taken from receiving an RFP to submitting a completed bid, an efficiency indicator of the bid preparation process.
kpis.formula: (Average Time from RFP Receipt to Bid Submission)
kpis.example: If 3 bids take 30, 40, and 50 hours for preparation, the average time to bid preparation is (30+40+50)/3 = 40 hours.

kpis.name: Time to Collect Payment From Clients
kpis.description: The average number of days it takes to receive payment after invoicing a client. Faster collection improves cash flow and indicates good client relations and efficient financial processes.
kpis.formula: (Total Days Taken to Collect Payment for all Projects / Number of Projects)
kpis.example: If in a month with 10 projects, it took a total of 250 days to collect all payments, the average time to collect would be 25 days.

kpis.name: Time To Complete Model Training
kpis.description: The duration taken to train an AI/ML model. Shorter training times can indicate efficient algorithms and optimized hardware utilization, although this varies by model complexity and data size.
kpis.formula: Duration from Training Start to Completion
kpis.example: If a model takes 5 hours to train, the time to complete model training is 5 hours.

kpis.name: Time To Deploy Completed Model
kpis.description: The time taken from when a model is fully trained until it is deployed in a production environment. This metric reflects the efficiency of the deployment process.
kpis.formula: Duration from Model Completion to Deployment
kpis.example: If a model takes 2 days from completion to being live in production, the time to deploy is 2 days.

kpis.name: Time To First API Call
kpis.description: Time To First API Call measures the duration from when a user first accesses the API to when they successfully make their first call. It’s an indicator of the ease of API integration and onboarding experience.
kpis.formula: Average Time from Access to First Successful API Call
kpis.example: If new users typically take 2 hours from accessing the API to making their first call, the time is 2 hours.

kpis.name: Time To First Purchase
kpis.description: Time To First Purchase tracks the average duration from a user's initial engagement (like signing up or installing) to making their first purchase. This metric is vital for understanding the sales cycle length and the effectiveness of conversion strategies. Shorter times to purchase can indicate a compelling value proposition and effective sales funnel.
kpis.formula: Average Time from Registration to First Purchase
kpis.example: If it typically takes users 7 days after registration to make their first purchase, the Time to First Purchase is 7 days.

kpis.name: Time to Hire
kpis.description:
The average duration taken from the day a job vacancy is announced to the day a successful candidate is selected. It encompasses the entirety of the recruitment process, from posting the job to interviewing and final selection.

A prolonged hiring process can lead to missed opportunities, as top candidates might get other offers. Additionally, it could indicate inefficiencies in the recruitment process or challenges related to specific roles. Being agile in hiring ensures that business functions aren't left understaffed for extended periods.
kpis.formula: (Total days to fill all positions / Number of positions filled)
kpis.example: If it takes on average 30 days to fill a position, the time to hire is 30 days.

kpis.name: Time to Learn From Incidents
kpis.description: Measures how quickly teams analyze and derive learnings from incidents, crucial for improving systems and processes to prevent future occurrences.
kpis.formula: (Time from Incident Resolution to Learning Implementation)
kpis.example: If learnings from an incident are implemented in 7 days, the time to learn is 7 days.

kpis.name: Time to Merge
kpis.description: The average duration from when a pull request is opened until it is merged. Shorter times can indicate more efficient review processes or simpler changes.
kpis.formula: (Total Time to Merge All PRs / Number of PRs)
kpis.example: If 10 PRs take an average of 2 days each to merge, the time to merge is 2 days.

kpis.name: Time to Merge
kpis.description: Reflects the average duration from when a pull request is opened until it is merged. This metric indicates the efficiency of the code review and integration process.
kpis.formula: (Total Time to Merge All PRs / Number of PRs)
kpis.example: If 10 PRs take an average of 2 days each to merge, the time to merge is 2 days.

kpis.name: Time to Start
kpis.description: The duration between a project agreement being finalized and the actual commencement of the project. Delays in project initiation can lead to customer dissatisfaction and may affect overall project timelines. A reduced time to start can increase client trust and boost the consultancy's efficiency.
kpis.formula: Date of project start - Date of project agreement
kpis.example: If a project agreement was made on the 1st of a month and the project began on the 5th, the Time to Start is 4 days.

kpis.name: Times Interest Earned Ratio
kpis.description: Measures a company's ability to meet its interest obligations from operations. A higher TIER suggests that the company is comfortably covering its interest expenses, reducing risk for lenders.
kpis.formula: EBIT / Interest Expense
kpis.example: If a company has an EBIT of $300,000 and interest expenses of $100,000, the TIER would be 3 times.

kpis.name: To-Complete Performance Index
kpis.description: To-Complete Performance Index (TCPI) is a measure of the cost performance required to meet a specified management goal or Budget at Completion (BAC). It indicates the cost efficiency needed for the remainder of the project.
kpis.formula: TCPI = (Budget at Completion (BAC) - Earned Value (EV)) / (Estimate at Completion (EAC) - Actual Cost (AC))
kpis.example: If BAC is $100,000, EV is $40,000, EAC is $110,000, and AC is $50,000, the TCPI is 0.75, meaning the project must run at 75% of the planned cost efficiency to stay on budget.

kpis.name: Toil Reduction
kpis.description: Tracks the reduction in toil, which is the repetitive, manual work in system maintenance, over time. Reducing toil is key to improving efficiency and focusing on more strategic tasks.
kpis.formula: (Time Spent on Toil Before - Time Spent on Toil After)
kpis.example: If toil is reduced from 30 hours per week to 20 hours, the reduction is 30 - 20 = 10 hours.

kpis.name: Top Exit Pages
kpis.description: Pages from which users most frequently leave your website. Identifying these pages can help you understand potential issues in content or user experience.
kpis.formula: URL list ranked by count of exits
kpis.example: If your 'products' page has the highest number of exits, it's the top exit page.

kpis.name: Top Performing Affiliates
kpis.description: Affiliates generating the highest number of leads or sales. Helps in identifying and rewarding the most effective partners.
kpis.formula: Sorting affiliates based on the number of leads or sales generated
kpis.example: Affiliate A generated 100 sales this month, making them the top performer.

kpis.name: Top-Funnel Traffic
kpis.description: The amount of traffic or visitors at the initial stages of the customer journey. Provides a measure of how effective your outreach and awareness campaigns are.
kpis.formula: Total number of visitors during a specific period
kpis.example: 5,000 visitors in a month from various marketing campaigns.

kpis.name: Total Affiliate Partners
kpis.description: The total number of affiliates or partners promoting your product or service. A larger affiliate base often leads to increased reach and sales.
kpis.formula: Total count of registered affiliates or partners
kpis.example: If 150 individuals or entities have signed up as affiliates, your total affiliate partners count is 150.

kpis.name: Total Assets
kpis.description: The total amount of assets, both current and long-term, that a company possesses. Gives a comprehensive overview of the company's resources and financial position.
kpis.formula: Sum of all Current and Long-term Assets
kpis.example: If a company has $500,000 in current assets and $2,500,000 in long-term assets, total assets amount to $3,000,000.

kpis.name: Total Attendees
kpis.description: The total number of individuals who attend the event. Indicates the reach and potential impact of the event.

kpis.name: Total Audit Cost per Year
kpis.description: Quantifies the overall cost associated with conducting audits over a year. Understanding audit costs helps in budgeting and ensuring that audits provide value relative to their expense.
kpis.formula: Sum of all audit-related costs for the year
kpis.example:
If a company spent $50,000 on audits over a year, the total audit cost is $50,000.

The costs might include internal auditor salaries & benefits, external audit fees, audit software & tools, travel & accomodation, training & development, consultancy fees, administrative costs, post-audit activities, and potential penalties.

kpis.name: Total Deviation of Time in Projects
kpis.description: Total Deviation of Time sums the disparities between planned and actual timelines for all project tasks. It provides an aggregate view of project delays, guiding corrective actions.
kpis.formula: Total Deviation of Time = Sum of (Actual task completion time - Planned task completion time) for all tasks
kpis.example: If Task A is 2 days late, Task B is 1 day early, and Task C is 3 days late, the total deviation is 4 days.

kpis.name: Total HR-related Expenses
kpis.description: The cumulative costs associated with recruiting, salaries, benefits, training, and other HR activities. Helps in gauging employee-related expenditures against productivity or revenue.
kpis.formula: Sum of salaries + benefits + training costs + recruitment costs + other HR expenses
kpis.example: If total salaries are $500,000, benefits cost $50,000, and training expenses are $10,000, the total HR-related expenses would be $560,000.

kpis.name: Total Issues Reported
kpis.description: The total number of issues reported in the project. It reflects the community's involvement in identifying potential improvements or problems, crucial for project evolution and quality.
kpis.formula: Count of Issues Reported
kpis.example: If 300 issues are reported over a period, the total count is 300.

kpis.name: Total Liabilities
kpis.description: The total amount of debts and obligations owed by the company. Offers insights into the company's obligations and helps gauge its financial risk.
kpis.formula: Sum of all Current and Long-term Liabilities
kpis.example: If a company owes $200,000 in short-term liabilities and $1,000,000 in long-term debts, the total liabilities amount to $1,200,000.

kpis.name: Total Monthly Cost of Running a Blog
kpis.description: quantifies the total financial expenditure associated with maintaining and producing content for a blog over a month. This can be juxtaposed against the revenue or leads generated from the blog to determine its ROI.
kpis.formula: Hosting fees + Content Creation Costs + Promotional Costs + Advertising + Software/Tool Costs + Any other related costs
kpis.example: If a company spends $100 on hosting, $12000 on content creation, $2000 on advertising and promotions, and $900 on various blogging tools, the total monthly cost would be $15000.

kpis.name: Total Monthly Cost of Running a Podcast
kpis.description: The total monthly costs associated with running the podcast, including all input costs. This helps you understand whether the podcast is financially sustainable.
kpis.formula: Sum of all input costs per month
kpis.example: If the sum of all input costs for a month is $2000, the total monthly cost would be $2000.

kpis.name: Total Outstanding Payable Amount
kpis.description: The total amount that the company owes to its suppliers at a specific point in time. Indicates the company's short-term liquidity position and its ability to cover its current liabilities.
kpis.formula: Sum of all unpaid invoices.

kpis.name: Total Payroll Taxes
kpis.description: The sum of all payroll taxes paid by the organization. Monitoring this ensures that the company is compliant with tax obligations and helps in budgeting and financial forecasting.
kpis.formula: Sum of all payroll taxes (employer and withheld from employees).
kpis.example: If a company pays $200,000 in employer payroll taxes and withholds another $300,000 from employee salaries, the total payroll taxes would be $500,000.

kpis.name: Total Podcast Monthly Revenue
kpis.description: The total revenue generated by the podcast in a given month. This is a direct measure of the podcast's profitability and success.

kpis.name: Total Reimbursed Expenses
kpis.description: The total amount reimbursed to employees for business-related expenses. Helps in monitoring the cash outflow related to employee expenditures and ensures that reimbursements are within company policy and budget.
kpis.formula: Sum of all reimbursed amounts within a specified period.
kpis.example: If employees submitted expenses totaling $100,000 in a quarter, then the total reimbursed expenses would be $100,000 for that quarter.

kpis.name: Total Tax Paid
kpis.description: The total amount of taxes paid during a specific period. This metric provides a straightforward representation of a company's tax obligation and can be compared to prior periods to track fluctuations.
kpis.formula: Sum of all taxes paid within the period
kpis.example: If a company pays $50,000 in federal taxes, $20,000 in state taxes, and $10,000 in local taxes, the Total Tax Paid is $80,000.

kpis.name: Training Completion Rate
kpis.description:
The percentage of employees who complete assigned or offered training sessions and courses within a specified timeframe.

Training programs are crucial for skill development and growth. A high training completion rate indicates that employees are engaged, see value in the training, and are equipped with the necessary skills for their roles. Low rates may hint at problems in training content, delivery, or overall relevance.
kpis.formula: (Number of employees who completed training / Total number of employees enrolled) x 100
kpis.example: If out of 200 enrolled employees, 180 complete their training, the completion rate is 90%.

kpis.name: Training Sessions Conducted Per Year
kpis.description: Counts the total number of compliance training sessions held within a specified year. Regular training sessions are critical in keeping employees updated on the latest compliance guidelines.
kpis.formula: Count of training sessions logged in the training calendar
kpis.example: If the organization conducts 25 training sessions in a year, the KPI would be 25.

kpis.name: Trial Conversion Rate
kpis.description: Trial Conversion Rate is pivotal for freemium or trial-based software products, indicating the percentage of users converting from a free trial to a paid subscription. It measures the product’s ability to demonstrate value compelling enough to convince users to pay. This metric helps in evaluating the effectiveness of the trial experience and pricing strategies.
kpis.formula: (Number of Users Converting to Paid / Total Users in Trial) * 100
kpis.example: If 200 out of 1,000 trial users upgrade to a paid plan, the Trial Conversion Rate is (200/1000)*100 = 20%.

kpis.name: Unauthorized Access Attempts Detected
kpis.description: Measures the number of detected attempts to access systems, applications, or data without the necessary authorization. Monitoring and responding to unauthorized access attempts is crucial for data protection and overall cybersecurity.
kpis.formula: Count of unauthorized access attempts logged
kpis.example: If there were 50 unauthorized access attempts detected in a month, the KPI would be 50.

kpis.name: Unique API Consumers
kpis.description: Unique API Consumers counts the distinct users or services that make requests to the API. It's a key metric for understanding the API's reach and diversity of its user base, indicative of its popularity and utility.
kpis.formula: Count of Distinct Users or Services Making Requests
kpis.example: If 500 different clients use the API over a period, the number of Unique API Consumers is 500.

kpis.name: Unsubscribe Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of recipients who opt-out of your email list. A high rate can indicate irrelevant or overly frequent content.
kpis.formula: (Number of Unsubscribes / Number of Emails Sent) x 100
kpis.example: If you send 1,000 emails and 20 people unsubscribe, the rate is 2%.

kpis.name: Upsell and Cross-sell Training Efficacy
kpis.description: Measures how effective training is at improving upsell and cross-sell techniques by comparing sales figures before and after training sessions.
kpis.formula: (Post-Training Upsell/Cross-sell Sales - Pre-Training Sales) / Pre-Training Sales * 100
kpis.example: If pre-training sales are $50,000 and post-training sales are $70,000, the efficacy is ($70,000-$50,000)/$50,000*100 = 40%.

kpis.name: Upsell/Cross-sell Success Rate
kpis.description: Tracks the effectiveness of upselling or cross-selling efforts within existing accounts.
kpis.formula: (Number of Successful Upsell/Cross-sell Deals / Number of Opportunities Presented) * 100
kpis.example: For 30 successful deals out of 100 opportunities, success rate is (30 / 100) * 100 = 30%.

kpis.name: Uptime Percentage of Critical Monitoring Systems
kpis.description: The operational uptime of essential monitoring systems in place to ensure compliance. Continuous monitoring is crucial for compliance; any downtime can mean undetected issues and potential non-compliance risks.
kpis.formula: (Operational time of monitoring systems / Total time) x 100
kpis.example: If a system was operational for 360 days out of a 365-day year, the uptime percentage would be 98.63%.

kpis.name: User Churn Rate
kpis.description: User Churn Rate quantifies the percentage of users who stop using the software over a specific period. It's a critical indicator of user satisfaction and product-market fit. A high churn rate can signal underlying issues with the product or service, requiring attention to aspects such as user experience, feature set, or customer service. Conversely, a low churn rate is indicative of strong user loyalty and product satisfaction.
kpis.formula: (Number of Users Who Churn / Total Users at Start of Period) * 100
kpis.example: If 100 out of 2,000 users stop using an app over a month, the churn rate is (100/2000)*100 = 5%.

kpis.name: User Error Rate
kpis.description: User Error Rate calculates the frequency of errors made by users while interacting with the product. High error rates may indicate complex or confusing interfaces, pointing to areas where UX improvements are needed.
kpis.formula: (Number of Errors Made / Total Number of Attempts) * 100
kpis.example: If users make 10 errors in 100 attempts to use a feature, the User Error Rate is (10/100)*100 = 10%.

kpis.name: User Growth Rate
kpis.description: User Growth Rate measures the rate at which the software's user base is expanding over time. It's an important indicator of the product's market traction and growth strategy effectiveness. Rapid user growth can be a sign of strong market demand, successful marketing initiatives, or product excellence, whereas stagnant or declining growth may warrant a review of market strategies or product offerings.
kpis.formula: ((Number of Users at End of Period - Number of Users at Start of Period) / Number of Users at Start of Period) * 100
kpis.example: If the user base grows from 10,000 to 12,000 in a month, the growth rate is ((12,000-10,000)/10,000)*100 = 20%.

kpis.name: User Retention Rate
kpis.description: User Retention Rate measures the proportion of users who continue using the software after a certain period, signifying long-term user engagement and satisfaction. It’s crucial for assessing the lasting appeal of the product and the effectiveness of retention strategies. High retention rates are indicative of a successful user experience and product-market fit, while low rates suggest areas for improvement in user engagement or product features.
kpis.formula: (Number of Users Still Active at End of Period / Number of Users at Start of Period) * 100
kpis.example: If 1,800 out of 2,000 users are still active after a month, the retention rate is (1800/2000)*100 = 90%.

kpis.name: Utility and Rent Expenses
kpis.description: The total expenditure for utilities (like water, electricity) and renting or leasing physical spaces. A fixed cost that can significantly impact profitability, especially for businesses that require large physical locations.
kpis.formula: Sum of all utility bills + rent or lease payments
kpis.example: If monthly rent is $20,000 and utilities cost $5,000, the combined monthly expense is $25,000.

kpis.name: Value of Items Stolen from Inventory
kpis.description: The total value of inventory items stolen. High values may indicate a need for better security measures.
kpis.formula: Total Value of Stolen Inventory Items
kpis.example: If items worth $10,000 are stolen from the inventory, the value is $10,000.

kpis.name: Value of Obsolete Stock
kpis.description: This KPI measures the monetary value of stock that's no longer relevant or sellable. High values can indicate purchasing or forecasting inefficiencies.
kpis.formula: Total Monetary Value of Items Considered Obsolete
kpis.example: If items considered obsolete sum up to a value of $5,000, then the value of obsolete stock is $5,000.

kpis.name: Variance At Completion
kpis.description: Variance At Completion (VAC) is the projected cost variance at project completion. It's a prediction of the expected difference between the budgeted and actual costs at the end of the project.
kpis.formula: VAC = Budget at Completion (BAC) - Estimate at Completion (EAC)
kpis.example: If the BAC is $240,000 and the EAC is $250,000, the VAC is -$10,000, indicating an expected overrun.

kpis.name: Video Click-Through Rate
kpis.description: Percentage of viewers who clicked on a CTA or link related to the video. Measures the effectiveness of CTAs and the video's ability to drive desired actions.
kpis.formula: (Number of Clicks / Number of Video Views) x 100
kpis.example: If a video had 1,000 views and 50 clicks on its CTA, the CTR is 5%.

kpis.name: Video Conversion Rate
kpis.description: Percentage of viewers who take a desired action (e.g., sign-up, purchase) after watching the video. Gauges the video's effectiveness in driving business goals.
kpis.formula: (Number of Conversions / Number of Video Views) x 100
kpis.example: If a video with 1,000 views results in 50 sign-ups, its conversion rate is 5%.

kpis.name: Video Production Cost
kpis.description: The total expenditure related to creating a video, including equipment, staff, locations, etc. Helps in budget allocation and understanding profitability.

kpis.name: Video Promotion Budget
kpis.description: Funds allocated to promote the video through various channels. Helps in gauging investment in video marketing and ROI calculations.

kpis.name: Video Views
kpis.description: The total number of times a video or all published videos have been watched. Gives an initial indicator of the video's or channel's popularity and reach.

kpis.name: Voluntary Turnover Rate
kpis.description:
The percentage of employees who willingly leave the company, either by resignation or retirement, during a specified time frame.

High voluntary turnover can signify employee dissatisfaction or more attractive opportunities elsewhere. It's essential for companies to understand the reasons for voluntary exits to address potential shortcomings.
kpis.formula: (Number of voluntary exits during the period / Average number of employees during the period) x 100
kpis.example: In a company with an average of 500 employees during the year, if 25 employees resign voluntarily, the voluntary turnover rate is 5%.

kpis.name: Website Conversion Rate
kpis.description: The percentage of visitors who take a desired action, like making a purchase or signing up for a newsletter. Directly correlates to website effectiveness in achieving its primary goals.
kpis.formula: (Number of conversions / Total visitors) x 100
kpis.example: If 100 out of 2,000 visitors made a purchase, the conversion rate is 5%.

kpis.name: Website Traffic
kpis.description: Measures the total number of visits to your website. Higher website traffic generally leads to increased brand awareness and potential sales.

kpis.name: Website Traffic From Email
kpis.description: The number of visitors to your website who arrived via a link in your email. Shows the role of email in driving traffic to your website.

kpis.name: Win/Loss Ratio After Bid Submission
kpis.description: Measures the ratio of won to lost bids after submission, offering insight into post-submission effectiveness and the strength of follow-up strategies.
kpis.formula: (Number of Won Bids / Number of Lost Bids)
kpis.example: If there are 15 won bids and 35 lost bids, the win/loss ratio is 15/35 ≈ 0.43.

kpis.name: Work In Progress (WIP)
kpis.description: Work In Progress (WIP) measures the number of tasks or deliverables currently being worked on within a project. It helps teams identify bottlenecks and inefficiencies, ensuring that no task remains stagnant or overlooked.
kpis.formula: WIP = Number of tasks/deliverables currently being worked on
kpis.example: If there are 15 tasks currently being tackled in a project, the WIP is 15.

kpis.name: Working Capital
kpis.description: The difference between a company's current assets and current liabilities. Positive working capital ensures that a company can continue its operations and meet short-term obligations.
kpis.formula: Current Assets - Current Liabilities
kpis.example: If a company has current assets of $1,000,000 and current liabilities of $500,000, its working capital is $500,000.

kpis.name: Year-Over-Year (YOY) Sales Growth
kpis.description: YOY Sales Growth compares sales figures from one year to the next, indicating long-term sales trends and seasonal impacts.
kpis.formula: (Current Year Sales - Previous Year Sales) / Previous Year Sales * 100
kpis.example: If last year sales were $1 million and this year sales are $1.2 million, YOY growth is ((1.2M-1M)/1M)*100 = 20%.
